title: Split a monolithic orders table into hash-partitioned shards without downtime
business_context: A logistics SaaS handles 1.4B rows in a single Postgres `orders` table. Autovacuum can no longer keep up, p99 on the dispatch query crossed 900ms, and a single bad index rebuild locked the table for 40 minutes last quarter. Ops wants 16 hash partitions by `tenant_id` so vacuum and index maintenance run per-partition.
constraints: Zero write downtime; 7k orders/sec peak ingest from 4 app instances
Postgres 14, logical replication available but no superuser on the prod replica
Existing FKs from `shipments` and `invoices` reference `orders.id`
Backfill of 1.4B rows must not exceed 30% extra disk for more than 6 hours
Rollback must be possible at every stage with a single config flip
Reads must keep working against old and new shape during cutover

before_state.description: One huge unpartitioned table; all maintenance is global and blocking.

issues: Single physical table: VACUUM and REINDEX lock 1.4B rows at once
No partition pruning, dispatch scan reads across all tenants
CREATE INDEX without CONCURRENTLY froze writes for 40m
Hardcodes the physical table name, so it can't target a partitioned parent transparently
No statement_timeout, a slow scan can pile up connections

reasoning: Naive approach: `ALTER TABLE orders PARTITION BY HASH` in place — impossible, Postgres can't convert an existing populated table to partitioned without a rewrite that takes an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock for the whole table. Rejected alternative: pg_dump/restore into a new partitioned table during a maintenance window — rejected because 1.4B rows take ~9h to restore and the business has no 9h window. Chosen path: create a NEW partitioned parent `orders_p`, attach 16 hash partitions, dual-write via a trigger on `orders`, backfill in keyset-paginated batches, then flip reads. Cross-layer trace: API repo keeps calling a view name `orders` -> view points at old table during backfill, swaps to `orders_p` at cutover -> data layer uses an INSERT trigger to mirror new writes into `orders_p` so no row is lost -> async backfill worker copies historical rows in 50k chunks ordered by id -> config flag `ORDERS_READ_SOURCE` picks view target -> tests assert row parity and FK integrity -> rollout ramps reads partition by tenant cohort.

after_state.description: Partitioned parent with 16 hash partitions; a view abstracts the physical source; dual-write keeps both in sync until cutover.

files.purpose: Defines the target hash-partitioned table so vacuum/reindex run per partition
Mirrors every live write into the partitioned table so the backfill only needs historical rows

files.content:
-- keyset-paginated backfill, run by worker in 50k chunks
INSERT INTO orders_p(id,tenant_id,status,total_cents,created_at)
SELECT id,tenant_id,status,total_cents,created_at FROM orders
WHERE id > $1 ORDER BY id LIMIT 50000
ON CONFLICT (id,tenant_id) DO NOTHING;
files.purpose: Copies pre-trigger rows in bounded chunks to cap disk/IO pressure
Atomically repoints the logical name the app uses from legacy table to partitioned parent
Targets the stable view name and adds a statement timeout to protect the pool

migration_strategy: Ship `orders_p` + 16 partitions and the dual-write trigger; verify new writes land in both (flag OFF for reads)
Run backfill worker in 50k keyset chunks during off-peak; monitor replication lag and disk
Canary: route 1% of tenants' reads to `orders_p` via the view target flag and compare row counts
Ramp reads to 100% by tenant cohort; keep legacy table writable as fallback
Cutover: rename legacy out, point `orders_rw` view at `orders_p` in one transaction
Cleanup: drop trigger and legacy table after 7 days of clean parity audits
testing_strategy: Seed a fixture of 2M rows across 5 tenants with known status mix. Assert dispatchable() output equals between legacy and partitioned sources (output parity) AND assert the trigger side-effect: every INSERT into orders produces a matching row in the correct partition (query pg_class to confirm partition routing). Chaos test: kill the backfill worker mid-chunk and re-run, asserting ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING yields zero duplicate ids and no FK violation in shipments.
what_breaks_naive_approach: In-place `ALTER TABLE ... PARTITION BY` is not supported on a populated table; even the dump/restore path holds writes for ~9h and any FK from shipments to orders.id breaks the moment the table is renamed without a view indirection.

title: Add a NOT NULL column with a default to a 300M-row table without locking writes
business_context: A fintech ledger service must add `currency` to `transactions` (300M rows) because a new EU market launches in 3 weeks. A junior tried `ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN currency TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'USD'` on Postgres 10 in staging and locked the table for 11 minutes. Prod runs Postgres 11 but the team is unsure the fast-default optimization applies with their volatile default.
constraints: 12k txn/sec sustained; any ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock over 2s trips the SLO
Backward-compat: old app pods (no currency) must keep inserting during rollout
No NULLs may ever be readable by the reconciliation job
Default must eventually be tenant-specific, not a constant
Rollback to nullable must be one statement
Multi-instance: 6 API pods on rolling deploy over 20 minutes

before_state.description: Transactions table lacks currency; a naive single-statement add would rewrite the table.

issues: No currency column; reconciliation assumes USD implicitly
Adding NOT NULL+volatile default forces a full table rewrite under exclusive lock
No currency on insert; new and old pods would diverge if column becomes NOT NULL mid-deploy

reasoning: Naive approach: one `ADD COLUMN currency TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT lookup_currency(account_id)` — a volatile/function default defeats Postgres 11's constant fast-default and rewrites all 300M rows under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE. Rejected alternative: add NOT NULL with constant 'USD' default then UPDATE — rejected because the constant is wrong for EU accounts and a 300M-row UPDATE bloats the table and still needs a follow-up rewrite. Chosen path: three-phase expand/migrate/contract. Cross-layer trace: API insert.js starts writing currency (defaulting to account's currency) BEFORE the column is mandatory -> add column NULLABLE (instant, no rewrite) -> backfill currency in batched UPDATEs by account ranges -> add a NOT VALID check constraint then VALIDATE concurrently -> finally SET NOT NULL using the validated constraint to skip a full scan -> config gates the validation step -> tests assert no NULLs leak to reconciliation.

after_state.description: Column added nullable, backfilled in batches, then made NOT NULL via a pre-validated constraint so no full-table exclusive scan occurs.

files.content: ALTER TABLE transactions ADD COLUMN currency TEXT; -- instant, no rewrite
files.purpose: Adds the column with no default so the catalog change is metadata-only
Writes a correct per-account currency from day one so backfill only covers history

files.content:
-- batched by account_id range, run repeatedly until 0 rows
UPDATE transactions t SET currency = a.currency
FROM accounts a
WHERE t.account_id = a.id AND t.currency IS NULL
  AND t.account_id BETWEEN $1 AND $2;
files.purpose: Fills historical NULLs in bounded ranges to avoid one giant UPDATE
Promotes to NOT NULL without an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE full scan by leveraging a validated CHECK

migration_strategy: Deploy app change that writes currency on every insert (column still nullable)
Run ADD COLUMN nullable migration (metadata-only)
Backfill in account-range batches off-peak until zero NULLs remain
Add NOT VALID check, then VALIDATE CONCURRENTLY-style under SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE
SET NOT NULL leveraging the validated constraint, then drop the helper constraint
Rollback: ALTER COLUMN currency DROP NOT NULL is a single instant statement
testing_strategy: Fixture: 1M transactions across USD and EUR accounts with 30% NULL currency simulating history. Assert output: reconciliation query returns zero NULL currencies after backfill. Assert side-effect: VALIDATE step acquires SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE (not ACCESS EXCLUSIVE) by checking pg_locks during the migration. Negative test: run an old-pod insert (no currency) concurrently during the deploy window and assert it still succeeds because the column is nullable until step 5.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A volatile/function default on ADD COLUMN bypasses the fast-default optimization and rewrites all 300M rows under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE, blocking the 12k/sec write stream for minutes and tripping the SLO.

title: Change a primary key from INT to BIGINT on a near-overflow events table
business_context: A gaming analytics platform's `events` table uses INT4 ids and is at 1.9B of the 2.147B INT4 ceiling, growing 40M rows/day — roughly 6 days from a hard insert failure. The table is 240GB and referenced by `event_tags`. A direct `ALTER COLUMN id TYPE BIGINT` would rewrite all 240GB under an exclusive lock.
constraints: Less than 6 days of headroom; cannot take a long maintenance window
55k events/sec ingest from Kafka consumers across 8 pods
FK from event_tags.event_id must stay valid throughout
Postgres 13; pg_repack available
Replica must stay in sync; logical decoding slot exists
Any rollback must not lose events accepted after cutover

before_state.description: INT4 surrogate key approaching overflow with a dependent FK.

issues: INT4 id at 1.9B will overflow in ~6 days
Direct ALTER TYPE BIGINT rewrites 240GB under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE
FK column event_tags.event_id is also INT4 and must change in lockstep

reasoning: Naive approach: `ALTER TABLE events ALTER COLUMN id TYPE BIGINT` — rewrites the entire 240GB heap and all indexes under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE for hours; the FK forces event_tags to rewrite too. Rejected alternative: pg_repack to widen the column — pg_repack copies type as-is and cannot change the column type, so it doesn't help. Chosen path: add a new `id_big BIGINT` column, backfill from id, sync via trigger, build a unique index concurrently, then swap the primary key and FK in a short transaction. Cross-layer trace: Kafka consumer keeps inserting (sequence now feeds id_big) -> trigger copies id into id_big for any path still writing id -> backfill UPDATE fills historical id_big -> CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY on id_big -> short txn drops old PK, promotes id_big unique index to PK, redefines FK -> config switches sequence ownership -> tests assert FK integrity and no id collision.

after_state.description: BIGINT id_big backfilled and promoted to primary key; FK repointed; old INT column dropped after verification.

files.content:
ALTER TABLE events ADD COLUMN id_big BIGINT; -- metadata only
ALTER TABLE event_tags ADD COLUMN event_id_big BIGINT;
files.purpose: Adds wide columns without rewriting the heap
Keeps id_big populated for any write that still sets only id
Populates wide columns in batches and builds indexes without blocking writes
Promotes the BIGINT column to PK and rewires the FK in one short transaction

migration_strategy: Add nullable BIGINT columns on events and event_tags (metadata-only)
Install BEFORE trigger so live inserts populate id_big from id
Backfill historical rows in id ranges, then build unique/FK indexes CONCURRENTLY
Swap PK to id_big and repoint FK inside one short transaction during low traffic
Reset the identity sequence to OWNED BY the new BIGINT id and continue from current max
Rollback before step 4: drop the trigger and new columns; the original INT PK is untouched
testing_strategy: Fixture: 5M events plus 8M event_tags with referential links and ids near 2.1B. Assert output: SELECT count(*) FROM event_tags et JOIN events e ON et.event_id=e.id equals pre-migration join count (FK integrity). Assert side-effect: the identity sequence's last_value is greater than max(id) so no collision on next insert. Chaos test: insert events on 8 simulated pods during the swap transaction and assert they queue briefly then commit with unique ids, no overflow.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A direct ALTER COLUMN TYPE BIGINT rewrites 240GB and every dependent index under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE for hours, while the FK forces event_tags into the same rewrite — far longer than the 6-day overflow runway allows.

title: Build a composite index online to fix a slow tenant-scoped search query
business_context: A B2B SaaS support tool runs `SELECT ... FROM tickets WHERE org_id=$1 AND status=$2 ORDER BY updated_at DESC LIMIT 50`. With 80M tickets the query does a 1.2s bitmap scan because only single-column indexes exist. Adding the right composite index naively with `CREATE INDEX` would lock writes on a table taking 900 inserts/sec.
constraints: No write downtime; 900 inserts/sec, 3 app pods
Postgres 15
Index build must be resumable if the connection drops
Disk headroom is 18% — index must fit
Query must use index-only scan for the LIMIT 50 hot path
Rollback must not leave an invalid index visible to the planner

before_state.description: Only single-column indexes exist; the planner bitmap-ANDs them and sorts, costing 1.2s.

files.content:
CREATE INDEX idx_tickets_org ON tickets(org_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_tickets_status ON tickets(status);
issues: No composite to satisfy org_id+status+order-by
Bitmap AND of two indexes then sort scans tens of thousands of rows
updated_at not in any index so ORDER BY needs an explicit sort
Relies on planner to combine indexes; p99 1.2s
No covering columns so heap fetch per row

reasoning: Naive approach: `CREATE INDEX idx_tickets_org_status_updated ON tickets(org_id,status,updated_at DESC)` — without CONCURRENTLY this takes an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE-equivalent share lock that blocks writes for the whole build on 80M rows. Rejected alternative: add updated_at to the existing org index — rejected because the status predicate would still require a heap filter, leaving the sort and heap fetches. Chosen path: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY with an INCLUDE for covering columns to enable index-only scans, then ANALYZE. Cross-layer trace: search.js query unchanged -> planner picks the new covering index -> data layer serves LIMIT 50 from the index leaf without heap fetch -> no async needed -> config: drop the redundant single-column indexes after verification -> tests assert plan node is Index Only Scan.

after_state.description: A concurrently-built covering composite index supports the predicate, ordering, and projection in one index-only scan.

files.purpose: Builds a covering index without blocking writes and refreshes stats for the planner

files.content:
-- only after verifying the new index is used
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF EXISTS idx_tickets_status;
files.purpose: Removes the now-redundant single-column status index to reclaim disk
Same SQL, now backed by a covering index; documents the expected plan

migration_strategy: Run CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY in a session with no surrounding transaction
If the build fails, DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY the leftover INVALID index and retry
ANALYZE and verify EXPLAIN shows Index Only Scan on a canary org
Monitor p99 for 24h before dropping redundant single-column indexes
Drop idx_tickets_status CONCURRENTLY to reclaim disk
Rollback: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY the new index; planner reverts to bitmap scan
testing_strategy: Fixture: 5M tickets across 200 orgs with realistic status skew. Assert output: recent() returns the 50 most-recent rows in correct order. Assert side-effect: EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) shows node type 'Index Only Scan' and Heap Fetches near zero. Negative test: start the CONCURRENTLY build, kill the session mid-build, and assert the leftover index is marked indisvalid=false and is ignored by the planner until dropped and rebuilt.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A plain CREATE INDEX takes a lock that blocks all INSERT/UPDATE on tickets for the full 80M-row build, stalling the 900/sec write stream for minutes.

title: Migrate MongoDB embedded order-items to a referenced collection for unbounded growth
business_context: A marketplace stores orders in MongoDB with line items embedded in the order document. Power sellers now create orders with 8k+ items, pushing documents past the 16MB BSON limit and causing failed writes during flash sales. The team must move items into a separate `order_items` collection without breaking the 40 services reading orders.
constraints: 16MB BSON hard limit already hit on 0.3% of orders
Zero read downtime across 40 consuming services
9k order reads/sec; embedded-shape readers must keep working during migration
MongoDB 6, replica set, no sharding yet
Backfill of 220M orders must be idempotent and resumable
Rollback must restore embedded reads instantly

before_state.description: Items embedded in the order doc; large orders exceed the BSON limit and fail.

issues: Unbounded items array breaches 16MB on large orders
Whole-document rewrites on every item update are O(items)
No way to paginate items independently
Returns the full embedded shape; consumers depend on order.items

reasoning: Naive approach: `$out` items into a new collection and immediately drop the embedded array — breaks all 40 services that read order.items synchronously. Rejected alternative: cap the array and reject >5k items — rejected because it loses legitimate bulk orders and pushes the failure to the business layer. Chosen path: introduce `order_items` collection, dual-write new items to both embedded array (capped) and the collection, backfill historical items idempotently, and expose a compatibility read that hydrates items from the collection. Cross-layer trace: write path appends to order_items and keeps a lightweight `itemCount` on the order -> read.js gains a hydrate option that $lookup-joins items so legacy callers still see order.items -> async backfill copies embedded arrays into order_items keyed by (orderId, sku) for idempotency -> config flag ORDER_ITEMS_SOURCE toggles embedded vs referenced -> tests assert shape parity and count integrity.

after_state.description: Items live in a referenced collection; a hydration layer preserves the embedded shape for legacy readers until they migrate.

files.purpose: Stores items independently with a unique (orderId,sku) key for idempotent backfill
Writes items to the new collection idempotently and tracks a count on the order
Preserves the embedded shape for legacy callers by hydrating from the collection
Idempotently copies embedded items into the new collection, resumable via the migratedItems flag

migration_strategy: Create order_items with unique (orderId,sku) index; deploy dual-write to both shapes
Backfill historical orders with bulk upserts, marking migratedItems=true per order
Enable hydration reads behind ORDER_ITEMS_SOURCE flag; canary 1% of traffic
Ramp hydration to 100% and stop writing to the embedded array (cap removed)
After consumers adopt order_items directly, drop the embedded items field
Rollback: set ORDER_ITEMS_SOURCE=embedded to serve the still-present embedded array
testing_strategy: Fixture: 100k orders including 50 with 8k+ items that previously failed. Assert output: getOrder(id) returns identical items (sorted by sku) whether served embedded or hydrated. Assert side-effect: order_items count equals embedded array length per order; itemCount field matches. Chaos test: run backfill twice and kill it midway; assert bulkWrite upserts produce no duplicate (orderId,sku) and migratedItems prevents reprocessing.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Dropping the embedded array immediately breaks all 40 services that read order.items, and the 16MB limit still blocks the largest orders during the $out copy if items are re-embedded.

title: Convert range-partition by month to declarative partitioning for an IoT readings table
business_context: An IoT platform ingests 2.1B sensor readings/month into a single Postgres table. Queries always filter by time range but scan the whole table; old data deletion via DELETE generates massive WAL and bloat. They need monthly range partitions with automatic creation and detach-based retention.
constraints: 18k readings/sec from MQTT bridge, 6 pods
Postgres 14
Retention: drop data older than 13 months without long locks
Time-range queries must prune to one or two partitions
Backfill of 27B existing rows must not duplicate
Rollback must keep historical queries working

before_state.description: Monolithic readings table; deletes cause bloat, no pruning.

issues: No partitioning; time-range scans read 27B rows
DELETE for retention bloats heap and floods WAL
Single index rebuild locks the whole table
No partition pruning; planner scans full index
Retention handled elsewhere by slow DELETE

reasoning: Naive approach: ALTER TABLE to partitioned in place — unsupported on a populated table. Rejected alternative: inheritance-based partitioning with manual CHECK constraints — rejected because constraint exclusion is weaker than native partition pruning and triggers add per-row overhead at 18k/sec. Chosen path: create native RANGE-partitioned parent, attach monthly partitions, dual-write via parent insert routing, backfill per-month with ATTACH of pre-built tables, and switch retention to DETACH+DROP. Cross-layer trace: query.js targets a view that points at the partitioned parent -> inserts route by ts automatically -> async pg_cron creates next month partition ahead of time -> backfill builds each month table offline then ATTACH PARTITION -> retention detaches old partitions instantly -> tests assert pruning to single partition.

after_state.description: Native range-partitioned parent with monthly partitions, automated creation, and detach-based retention.

files.purpose: Defines the range-partitioned parent and current partition with a local index
Pre-creates the upcoming partition so ingest never hits a missing range
Backfills a month into a standalone table then attaches it with only a brief metadata lock
Implements retention with an instant detach+drop, avoiding WAL-heavy DELETEs

migration_strategy: Create partitioned parent + current/next partitions; route writes through it behind a view
Backfill historical months by building standalone tables then ATTACH PARTITION
Schedule pg_cron to pre-create next month and to detach+drop beyond 13 months
Canary time-range reads against the parent; verify pruning via EXPLAIN
Cut all reads to the parent view; stop writing to the legacy table
Rollback: repoint view to legacy table; partitions remain readable independently
testing_strategy: Fixture: 50M readings across 3 months and 10k devices. Assert output: window() returns identical rows from legacy and partitioned sources. Assert side-effect: EXPLAIN shows only the matching month partition scanned (pruning) and DETACH leaves zero dead tuples versus a DELETE baseline measured via pg_stat_user_tables. Chaos test: ingest a reading whose ts falls in a not-yet-created future month and assert ensure_next_partition prevents the insert error.
what_breaks_naive_approach: In-place conversion to a partitioned table is unsupported on populated tables, and DELETE-based retention floods WAL and leaves bloat that vacuum can't reclaim fast enough at 18k/sec.

title: Introduce optimistic-lock version column to fix lost-update race in inventory
business_context: An e-commerce inventory service oversells during flash sales because two concurrent decrements read-then-write stock without locking. Last Black Friday 4,200 oversold units triggered refunds. The team must add optimistic concurrency without taking row locks that would serialize the 6k decrements/sec hot SKUs.
constraints: 6k decrements/sec on hot SKUs across 5 pods
No pessimistic SELECT FOR UPDATE (would serialize hot rows)
Backward-compat: old pods without version must not corrupt stock
Postgres 13
Decrement must be atomic and never go negative
Rollback to old code path must be safe mid-deploy

before_state.description: Read-modify-write decrement with a lost-update race.

issues: Read-then-write is non-atomic; two requests both read 5 and both write 3
No guard against negative stock under concurrency
Causes oversell during flash sales
No version column for optimistic concurrency
No CHECK preventing negative stock

reasoning: Naive approach: wrap the read-modify-write in SELECT FOR UPDATE — correct but serializes every decrement on a hot SKU, collapsing throughput at 6k/sec. Rejected alternative: application-level mutex in Redis — rejected because it adds a network round-trip per decrement and a Redis outage stalls all sales. Chosen path: make the decrement a single atomic conditional UPDATE guarded by a version and a stock>=qty predicate, retry on zero rows. Cross-layer trace: decrement.js issues one UPDATE ... WHERE sku AND stock>=qty RETURNING -> if zero rows, it lost the race or is oversold, so it re-reads and retries with backoff -> data layer adds a CHECK (stock>=0) as a backstop -> version increments per write so stale old-pod writes are rejected -> config: both old and new pods can run because the atomic UPDATE is also correct -> tests assert no oversell under concurrency.

after_state.description: Single atomic conditional decrement with a version guard and a non-negative CHECK; bounded retry on contention.

files.content:
ALTER TABLE inventory ADD COLUMN version BIGINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0;
ALTER TABLE inventory ADD CONSTRAINT stock_non_negative CHECK (stock >= 0);
files.purpose: Adds the optimistic version and a hard backstop preventing negative stock
Performs an atomic guarded decrement and retries only on lost-race contention, not on true oversell
Proves the atomic path eliminates lost updates under concurrency

migration_strategy: Add version column and stock>=0 CHECK (both metadata-safe, default 0)
Deploy atomic conditional decrement to all pods (works alongside old code)
Canary one SKU cohort, monitor oversell counter and contention retries
Ramp to 100%; remove old read-modify-write path
Add alert if contention retries exceed threshold per SKU
Rollback: revert app to prior version; the CHECK still blocks negative stock
testing_strategy: Fixture: SKU with stock 50, fire 100 concurrent decrements. Assert output: exactly 50 succeed and 50 throw 'oversold'. Assert side-effect: final stock is exactly 0 and version incremented exactly 50 times (no double-apply). Chaos test: kill a pod mid-decrement after the UPDATE committed; assert the client retry does not double-decrement because the committed row already reflects the change and the retry sees stock>=qty fail-or-pass correctly.
what_breaks_naive_approach: The read-then-write gap lets two requests both read the same stock and both write a decremented value, losing one decrement and overselling; SELECT FOR UPDATE fixes it but serializes hot SKUs and collapses throughput.

title: Migrate from single-region Postgres to Citus distributed sharding for write scale
business_context: A social platform's `posts` table hit single-node write ceiling at 22k inserts/sec; vertical scaling is exhausted on the largest instance. They must distribute `posts` and `post_likes` across a Citus cluster sharded by `user_id` so co-located joins stay local, without losing the existing 4.8B rows or breaking the timeline API.
constraints: 22k inserts/sec, write-bound on a single node
Co-locate posts and post_likes by user_id for local joins
Zero downtime; timeline reads must not break
FKs and unique constraints must include the distribution column
Backfill of 4.8B rows must be online
Rollback to single-node must be possible during ramp

before_state.description: Single-node Postgres at its write ceiling; joins are fast but the node is saturated.

issues: Single-node write ceiling reached at 22k/sec
PK on posts.id alone can't be a Citus distributed unique key without user_id
FK references posts(id) not the distribution column
Join not co-located if tables are distributed by different keys

reasoning: Naive approach: distribute posts by id and post_likes by post_id — breaks co-location, so the timeline join becomes a cross-shard repartition join, slow and chatty. Rejected alternative: shard by created_at time bucket — rejected because it creates write hotspots on the newest shard and scatters a user's posts across shards. Chosen path: distribute both tables by user_id, fold user_id into PKs/FKs/unique keys, use create_distributed_table with colocate_with, and backfill via Citus online table move. Cross-layer trace: timeline read.js filters by user_id so the query routes to a single shard -> co-located posts+post_likes join stays node-local -> distribution column user_id added to all keys -> backfill uses create_distributed_table on a shadow then swaps -> config: coordinator connection string -> tests assert single-shard routing.

after_state.description: Posts and post_likes distributed by user_id and co-located; keys include the distribution column; timeline join routes to one shard.

files.purpose: Distributes both tables by user_id and co-locates them so joins stay node-local
Repoints the FK to include the distribution column so Citus can enforce it locally
Adds user_id to the join condition so the query is single-shard and co-located

files.content:
const pool = new Pool({ connectionString: process.env.CITUS_COORDINATOR_URL, max: 50 });
module.exports = pool;
files.purpose: Points the app at the Citus coordinator; sharding is transparent to callers

migration_strategy: Stand up Citus cluster; rewrite keys to include user_id on a shadow schema
Use create_distributed_table to shard posts then post_likes co-located
Online-backfill via logical replication from single-node to coordinator
Canary timeline reads on the coordinator for an internal cohort; verify single-shard routing
Cut writes to the coordinator; keep single-node as warm standby
Rollback during ramp: repoint app to single-node which still has all rows
testing_strategy: Fixture: 10M posts and 30M likes across 50k users loaded into a 4-node test cluster. Assert output: timeline() returns the same rows/counts as single-node. Assert side-effect: EXPLAIN via citus shows 'Task Count: 1' (single-shard routing), not a repartition join. Chaos test: down one worker node and assert reads for users on surviving shards still succeed while the affected shard returns a clear error, proving blast radius is one shard.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Distributing the two tables by different keys destroys co-location, turning the per-user timeline join into a cross-shard repartition that fans out to every worker and is far slower than the single node it replaced.

title: Replace ENUM column with a lookup table to allow runtime status additions
business_context: A logistics app stores shipment `status` as a Postgres ENUM. Adding a new status requires ALTER TYPE which can't run in a transaction with other DDL and historically blocked deploys. Operations now need to add statuses weekly. The team must move to a reference table without breaking 12 services that compare status as text.
constraints: Status compared as text in 12 services — must stay text-compatible
Adding a status must be a plain INSERT, no DDL
Postgres 12; ALTER TYPE ADD VALUE can't run inside a multi-statement migration txn
3k status updates/sec
Backfill must preserve exact existing values
Rollback must restore ENUM behavior

before_state.description: ENUM-typed status that requires DDL to extend and blocks transactional migrations.

issues: Adding a status needs ALTER TYPE ADD VALUE which can't be in a txn with other DDL
ENUM ordering is fixed at creation; reordering needs a full type rebuild
Cross-service text comparisons couple to the ENUM label set
Passes text that must match an ENUM label exactly or errors at write time

reasoning: Naive approach: keep the ENUM and just ALTER TYPE ADD VALUE each week — each add is non-transactional DDL that has blocked atomic deploys and can't be rolled back inside a migration. Rejected alternative: switch the column to free-text TEXT with no constraint — rejected because it loses referential validation and lets typos like 'IN_TRANSIT ' corrupt analytics. Chosen path: create a `shipment_statuses` lookup table, change the column to TEXT with an FK to the lookup, seed it from the ENUM labels, and keep text comparisons working. Cross-layer trace: update.js still passes text -> the column becomes TEXT validated by an FK so invalid values are rejected at write time -> seed lookup from enum_range -> backfill is a no-op because labels are identical text -> adding a status becomes INSERT INTO shipment_statuses -> tests assert FK rejection of unknown status.

after_state.description: Status is TEXT validated by an FK to a lookup table; new statuses are plain inserts, text comparisons unchanged.

files.purpose: Creates and seeds the lookup table from the existing ENUM labels so values match exactly
Replaces the ENUM column with a text column validated by an FK, preserving exact values
Unchanged call signature; validation now enforced by the FK instead of the ENUM type
Lets operations add a status with a plain idempotent INSERT, no DDL or deploy

migration_strategy: Create and seed shipment_statuses from enum_range (labels identical to ENUM)
Add status_text column, backfill from status::text in batches
Add FK to lookup as NOT VALID, then VALIDATE without write block
Swap columns: drop ENUM column, rename status_text to status
Provide addStatus() so new statuses are inserts; drop the ENUM type after grace period
Rollback before swap: drop status_text and FK; ENUM column untouched
testing_strategy: Fixture: 2M shipments across all four statuses. Assert output: setStatus with a valid code succeeds and reads return the same text as before. Assert side-effect: FK constraint blocks an UPDATE to an unknown status code with a foreign_key_violation. Negative test: attempt addStatus then setStatus to the new code in the same test and assert it now succeeds without any DDL, proving the weekly-add pain is gone.
what_breaks_naive_approach: ALTER TYPE ADD VALUE cannot run inside a transaction alongside other DDL and is irreversible within a migration, so each weekly status add risks a half-applied, un-rollback-able deploy.

title: Backfill a denormalized counter column using a transactional outbox to avoid drift
business_context: A social app shows follower counts by running COUNT(*) on a 9B-row `follows` table per profile view, costing 600ms p99 and saturating read replicas. They want a denormalized `follower_count` on `users`, but a naive backfill plus trigger drifts because writes during backfill get double-counted or missed.
constraints: 9B follows rows; COUNT(*) per view is unsustainable
14k follow/unfollow events/sec
Counter must be eventually exact, drift < 0.01%
Backfill must not block follow writes
Postgres 14 + Kafka outbox
Rollback must fall back to COUNT(*) safely

before_state.description: Counts computed on read via COUNT(*), saturating replicas.

issues: COUNT(*) on a 9B-row table per profile view, 600ms p99
Saturates read replicas during traffic spikes
No counter maintained; every read recomputes
No outbox so downstream can't react to follow changes

reasoning: Naive approach: add a trigger that increments users.follower_count and run a one-shot backfill COUNT(*) UPDATE — the backfill and concurrent trigger increments overlap, so events during the backfill window get counted twice or missed, producing permanent drift. Rejected alternative: lock follows during backfill — rejected because it stalls 14k/sec writes. Chosen path: use a transactional outbox to emit follow/unfollow deltas, a consumer that applies deltas idempotently to the counter, and a snapshot backfill that records a watermark so only deltas after the snapshot are applied. Cross-layer trace: write.js inserts follow + outbox row in one txn -> consumer applies +1/-1 keyed by event id (idempotent) -> backfill computes COUNT as-of a watermark offset -> deltas before watermark are skipped, after are applied -> config flag picks counter vs COUNT(*) -> tests assert counter equals recomputed COUNT.

after_state.description: Counter maintained via an idempotent outbox consumer; backfill uses a Kafka watermark to avoid double counting.

files.purpose: Writes the follow and a delta outbox event atomically so no event is lost
Applies each delta exactly once using an applied_events dedupe table

files.content:
-- snapshot count as-of watermark; deltas after watermark replayed by consumer
UPDATE users u SET follower_count = sub.c
FROM (SELECT followee_id, count(*) c FROM follows GROUP BY followee_id) sub
WHERE u.id = sub.followee_id;
files.purpose: Sets the baseline from a consistent snapshot; the consumer reconciles post-snapshot deltas
Serves the denormalized counter with a flag to fall back to COUNT(*) for safety

migration_strategy: Add follower_count column (default 0) and applied_events dedupe table
Deploy atomic follow+outbox writes and the idempotent consumer (counter not yet read)
Run snapshot backfill; record the Kafka offset watermark at snapshot time
Consumer replays only post-watermark deltas so the counter converges to exact
Flip COUNTER_SOURCE to read the column; audit counter vs COUNT(*) nightly
Rollback: set COUNTER_SOURCE=compute to fall back to COUNT(*)
testing_strategy: Fixture: 1M follows seeded, then 50k follow/unfollow events fired during the backfill window. Assert output: followerCount() equals a fresh COUNT(*) for every user. Assert side-effect: applied_events contains each event id exactly once (no double apply) and the outbox is drained. Chaos test: redeliver the same Kafka batch twice and assert counters are unchanged on the second delivery, proving idempotency.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A trigger-plus-snapshot backfill double-counts or drops events that occur during the backfill window because there is no watermark, leaving the counter permanently drifted from the true COUNT.

title: Rename a high-traffic column via expand-and-contract without breaking readers
business_context: A healthcare scheduling service must rename `appointments.dt` to `scheduled_at` for clarity and FHIR alignment. The column is read/written by 18 services and a reporting pipeline. A direct RENAME would instantly break every service still referencing `dt` during the rolling deploy.
constraints: 18 services + 1 reporting pipeline read the column
Rolling deploy spans 25 minutes; old and new code coexist
2.5k writes/sec on appointments
Postgres 13
No data copy that doubles storage long-term
Rollback at any stage without data loss

before_state.description: Column dt is referenced everywhere; a rename would break in-flight readers.

issues: Column name dt is hardcoded in 18 services
Direct RENAME breaks all readers mid-deploy
Writes column dt; would error if column renamed before this pod updates

reasoning: Naive approach: ALTER TABLE RENAME COLUMN dt TO scheduled_at — instantaneous, but every still-deployed pod referencing dt errors immediately, causing a partial outage during the 25-minute rollout. Rejected alternative: create scheduled_at as a copy and dual-write from the app — rejected because it doubles storage on a hot column and requires perfect app-side sync. Chosen path: add scheduled_at as a GENERATED-like synced column via a view/trigger so both names work, migrate services one by one, then contract. Cross-layer trace: add scheduled_at column synced to dt via trigger both ways -> services migrate to scheduled_at one deploy at a time -> reporting pipeline switched last -> once no reader uses dt, drop the trigger and dt column -> config: no flag needed, both columns valid simultaneously -> tests assert both names return identical values during overlap.

after_state.description: Both column names coexist via a bidirectional sync trigger until all readers migrate, then dt is dropped.

files.purpose: Keeps dt and scheduled_at in lockstep so old and new code both write correctly
New code uses the new column name while the trigger maintains the old one
Removes the legacy column and sync machinery once no reader depends on dt

migration_strategy: Add scheduled_at, backfill from dt in batches, install bidirectional sync trigger
Deploy services one at a time to read/write scheduled_at (dt still synced)
Migrate the reporting pipeline last and verify it no longer queries dt
Audit query logs for any remaining reference to dt over 7 days
Contract: drop trigger, function, and dt column
Rollback before contract: dt column and trigger are intact, revert app deploys
testing_strategy: Fixture: 500k appointments with known dt values. Assert output: during overlap, SELECT dt and SELECT scheduled_at return identical timestamps for every row. Assert side-effect: a legacy-pod insert that sets only dt produces a row whose scheduled_at equals dt (trigger fired). Negative test: simulate a new-pod insert setting only scheduled_at and assert dt is populated so legacy readers don't see NULL.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A direct RENAME instantly invalidates every query in the 18 services still running old code during the 25-minute rollout, causing column-does-not-exist errors and a partial outage.

title: Move write-heavy session store from Postgres to Redis with dual-read fallback
business_context: A SaaS app keeps user sessions in a Postgres `sessions` table updated on every request for last-seen tracking, generating 40k UPDATEs/sec and 70% of all WAL. The team must move sessions to Redis with TTL while ensuring no user gets logged out during the transition.
constraints: 40k session UPDATEs/sec, 70% of WAL volume
No forced logouts during migration
Redis cluster with TTL; eviction policy noeviction for sessions
Backward-compat: old pods read Postgres sessions
Session validation must be correct under split-brain reads
Rollback to Postgres-only must be instant

before_state.description: Every request updates a Postgres sessions row, dominating WAL.

issues: 40k UPDATEs/sec on last_seen dominate WAL and bloat the table
Every request hits Postgres for validation, adding latency

reasoning: Naive approach: flip all reads/writes to Redis at once and truncate the Postgres table — any session not yet in Redis logs the user out, and a Redis outage during cutover logs everyone out. Rejected alternative: write sessions to both stores forever — rejected because it keeps the WAL problem and doubles write cost. Chosen path: dual-write to Redis with TTL, dual-read with Redis-first and Postgres fallback, lazy-migrate on read, then drop Postgres writes once hit rate is high. Cross-layer trace: session.js writes to Redis with TTL and stops Postgres last_seen UPDATEs -> get() reads Redis first, on miss reads Postgres and populates Redis (lazy migration) -> async no backfill job needed, traffic warms the cache -> config flag SESSION_STORE controls write target -> tests assert no session is lost on Redis miss.

after_state.description: Sessions in Redis with TTL; reads are Redis-first with Postgres fallback and lazy migration; Postgres writes removed.

files.purpose: Validates from Redis first, falls back to Postgres and populates Redis on miss
Creates sessions in Redis, optionally mirroring to Postgres during the dual phase for fallback

files.content: module.exports = { SESSION_STORE: process.env.SESSION_STORE || 'dual' }; // dual -> redis
files.purpose: Controls whether Postgres receives mirror writes, enabling instant rollback

migration_strategy: Deploy Redis dual-write on create + Redis-first read with Postgres fallback
Stop the 40k/sec last_seen UPDATEs; use Redis TTL refresh instead
Monitor Redis hit rate; lazy migration warms active sessions within one TTL
Switch SESSION_STORE to redis-only once hit rate > 99% and WAL drops
Keep Postgres table read-only as fallback for one TTL window, then drop it
Rollback: set SESSION_STORE=dual; Postgres still holds valid sessions
testing_strategy: Fixture: 100k Postgres sessions, empty Redis. Assert output: get() returns the correct user for a session present only in Postgres and subsequently serves it from Redis. Assert side-effect: after a get() miss, Redis contains the session hash with a TTL ~1800s (lazy migration). Chaos test: flush Redis mid-traffic and assert all requests still authenticate via Postgres fallback with zero forced logouts, then re-warm.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Cutting over to Redis-only and truncating Postgres logs out every user whose session hasn't yet been written to Redis, and a single Redis blip during cutover becomes a fleet-wide logout.

title: Shard a MySQL users table by hash with a routing layer and dual-write cutover
business_context: A fintech's MySQL `users` table reached 600GB on a single primary; replication lag spikes to 30s during peak writes, breaking read-after-write for KYC flows. They must shard users across 8 MySQL instances by hash(user_id) behind an application routing layer without downtime.
constraints: 600GB single primary; 30s replication lag at peak
Read-after-write must hold for KYC within the same shard
8 shards by hash(user_id); rebalancing not in scope
No global auto-increment; use snowflake-style ids
Backfill 180M users online
Rollback to single primary during ramp

before_state.description: Single MySQL primary saturated; lag breaks read-after-write.

issues: Single pool to one primary; no sharding
Auto-increment ids tie identity to one instance
Reads go to lagging replicas, breaking KYC read-after-write
AUTO_INCREMENT can't span shards
Unique email must stay globally unique across shards

reasoning: Naive approach: split the table by id ranges and route in app — range sharding creates a hot 'newest' shard since new ids cluster, and AUTO_INCREMENT collides across shards. Rejected alternative: a proxy like Vitess for transparent sharding — rejected for this milestone because operational adoption and resharding semantics exceed the timeline; they need a minimal routing layer now. Chosen path: hash(user_id) routing with snowflake ids generated app-side, a global uniqueness directory for email, dual-write during backfill, and read-after-write pinned to the shard primary. Cross-layer trace: routing layer maps user_id->shard via hash -> getUser reads the owning shard primary for KYC consistency -> snowflake id generator removes AUTO_INCREMENT dependence -> email uniqueness enforced via a small global lookup -> backfill copies each user to its hash shard -> tests assert correct shard routing and global email uniqueness.

after_state.description: Users sharded by hash(user_id) behind a routing layer; snowflake ids; global email directory; KYC reads hit the owning shard primary.

files.purpose: Routes by hash(user_id) and pins KYC read-after-write to the shard primary
Generates globally unique ids without cross-shard AUTO_INCREMENT coordination
Enforces global email uniqueness that per-shard UNIQUE constraints cannot
Copies each user to its hash-determined shard idempotently via INSERT IGNORE

migration_strategy: Deploy snowflake ids and email_directory; dual-write new users to source + target shard
Backfill historical users into hash shards via keyset pagination with INSERT IGNORE
Route reads through the router with KYC reads pinned to shard primaries
Canary a user cohort to shard reads; verify email uniqueness via the directory
Cut all traffic to shards; keep single primary as warm fallback
Rollback during ramp: point router at the single primary which still has all rows
testing_strategy: Fixture: 1M users distributed across 8 test shards. Assert output: getUser(id) returns the user from the correct shard for a sample spanning all 8 hash buckets. Assert side-effect: claimEmail rejects a duplicate email across two different shards via the global directory UNIQUE. Chaos test: create a user then immediately read with consistent=true and assert KYC status is present (read-after-write) even while replicas lag, by routing the read to the shard primary.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Range sharding concentrates all new signups on the highest-range shard creating a write hotspot, and per-shard AUTO_INCREMENT ids collide, corrupting identity across shards.

title: Add a partial unique index to enforce one active subscription per customer
business_context: A B2B SaaS billing bug let customers end up with two ACTIVE subscriptions after a retry race, causing double charges for 1,100 accounts. They must enforce at most one ACTIVE subscription per customer at the database level without blocking the 1,200 subscription writes/sec or rejecting historical CANCELED duplicates.
constraints: 1.2k subscription writes/sec, 4 pods
Only ACTIVE rows are constrained; CANCELED/PAST_DUE may repeat
Existing duplicate ACTIVE rows must be reconciled first
Index build must be online
Postgres 14
Rollback must not drop the de-dup fixes

before_state.description: No DB-level guarantee of a single active subscription; a retry race creates duplicates.

issues: No uniqueness on (customer_id) for ACTIVE status
Retry race inserts a second ACTIVE row -> double charge
Check-then-insert is racy; two concurrent calls both pass the check

reasoning: Naive approach: a full UNIQUE(customer_id) — wrong, because a customer legitimately has many CANCELED rows over time, so it would reject valid history. Rejected alternative: enforce uniqueness only in the app with a Redis lock — rejected because a lock outage or a missed code path reopens the double-charge hole; the DB must be the source of truth. Chosen path: a PARTIAL unique index WHERE status='ACTIVE', built CONCURRENTLY, after reconciling existing duplicates, and switch the insert to ON CONFLICT. Cross-layer trace: subscribe.js becomes an INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING on the partial unique index -> the DB rejects the second ACTIVE row atomically -> reconcile job cancels older duplicate ACTIVE rows first -> build partial index CONCURRENTLY -> config: no flag, the constraint is always-on once built -> tests assert second concurrent subscribe is rejected.

after_state.description: A partial unique index guarantees one ACTIVE subscription per customer; inserts use ON CONFLICT; duplicates reconciled.

files.purpose: Resolves existing duplicate ACTIVE rows so the unique index can be created

files.content:
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY uq_active_sub
  ON subscriptions(customer_id) WHERE status='ACTIVE';
files.purpose: Enforces at most one ACTIVE subscription per customer without constraining historical rows
Makes subscription creation atomic and idempotent against the partial unique index

migration_strategy: Run reconciliation to cancel older duplicate ACTIVE rows, keeping the newest
Build the partial unique index CONCURRENTLY (online)
Deploy subscribe() using ON CONFLICT against the partial index
Canary one billing cohort; verify retry races now no-op instead of double-inserting
Add a monitor for ON CONFLICT no-op rate to catch unexpected retry storms
Rollback: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY uq_active_sub; reconciliation fixes remain
testing_strategy: Fixture: customers each with a history of CANCELED rows plus the duplicate-ACTIVE bug for 1,100 of them. Assert output: after reconcile+index, each customer has exactly one ACTIVE row. Assert side-effect: two concurrent subscribe() calls for the same customer result in exactly one ACTIVE insert (rowCount 1 and 0). Negative test: assert inserting a CANCELED duplicate still succeeds, proving the partial predicate only constrains ACTIVE.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A full UNIQUE(customer_id) rejects the many legitimate CANCELED/PAST_DUE rows a customer accumulates, so it cannot be created and an app-only lock leaves the double-charge race open on any missed path.

title: Migrate JSONB blob columns to typed columns with a generated-column bridge
business_context: A devtools company stored build metadata in a single `meta JSONB` column. Queries filter on meta->>'branch' and meta->>'status' but can't use indexes efficiently, and a typo'd key silently returns nothing. With 1.1B build rows, analytics times out. They must promote hot keys to typed columns without breaking writers that send arbitrary JSON.
constraints: 1.1B build rows; analytics queries time out
Writers still send arbitrary JSON meta
Promoted columns must stay in sync with JSONB during transition
Index hot keys: branch, status, duration_ms
Postgres 13
Rollback to JSONB-only reads must work

before_state.description: All metadata in one JSONB column; no efficient indexing on hot keys.

issues: No typed columns for branch/status/duration
GIN index on meta is large and not selective for equality on single keys
Typos in key names silently match nothing
Expression predicates can't use a plain B-tree index
Analytics scans the JSONB on 1.1B rows

reasoning: Naive approach: add typed columns and require the app to write both JSONB and typed values — drifts whenever a writer forgets, and there are many writers sending arbitrary JSON. Rejected alternative: expression indexes on meta->>'branch' — rejected because they help equality lookups but not the analytics aggregations that need real typed columns, and they bloat with every key. Chosen path: STORED generated columns derived from the JSONB so they're always in sync automatically, index those, and migrate readers to the typed columns. Cross-layer trace: query.js switches to typed columns -> planner uses B-tree indexes on generated columns -> generated columns derive from meta so writers need no change -> backfill is implicit (generated STORED computes on rewrite; add via batched UPDATE to force) -> config flag picks typed vs JSONB read path -> tests assert generated value equals JSONB value.

after_state.description: Hot keys exposed as STORED generated columns derived from JSONB, indexed and queried directly; writers unchanged.

files.content:
ALTER TABLE builds
  ADD COLUMN branch TEXT GENERATED ALWAYS AS (meta->>'branch') STORED,
  ADD COLUMN status TEXT GENERATED ALWAYS AS (meta->>'status') STORED,
  ADD COLUMN duration_ms BIGINT GENERATED ALWAYS AS ((meta->>'duration_ms')::bigint) STORED;
files.purpose: Derives typed columns from JSONB automatically so they can never drift from writers

files.content:
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_builds_repo_branch_status ON builds(repo_id, branch, status);
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_builds_repo_duration ON builds(repo_id, duration_ms);
files.purpose: Indexes the typed columns so equality and range analytics use B-tree scans
Reads the indexed typed columns with a flag to fall back to JSONB expressions

migration_strategy: Add STORED generated columns (forces a rewrite on add; do per Postgres-version-appropriate batched approach or low-traffic window)
Build typed-column indexes CONCURRENTLY
Switch reads to typed columns behind BUILD_READ flag; canary analytics
Verify generated columns equal JSONB extraction on a sample audit
Drop the large GIN index on meta if it existed once typed reads are stable
Rollback: set BUILD_READ=jsonb; generated columns are harmless and stay in sync
testing_strategy: Fixture: 2M builds with varied branch/status/duration and some missing keys. Assert output: failedOnBranch returns the same ids via typed columns and via JSONB. Assert side-effect: for every row, branch column equals meta->>'branch' (generated correctness), including NULL where the key is absent. Negative test: write a build whose meta omits duration_ms and assert duration_ms is NULL (not an error), so analytics handles missing keys gracefully.
what_breaks_naive_approach: App-maintained duplicate columns drift whenever any of the many arbitrary-JSON writers forgets to set them, producing silently wrong analytics; generated columns remove that failure mode.

title: Introduce a foreign key on a large table without a long validating lock
business_context: A healthcare claims system has `claim_lines.claim_id` referencing claims, but no FK exists, so orphaned lines accumulated (0.4% of 800M rows) after a buggy bulk delete. They must add the FK to prevent future orphans without the validation scan locking writes on a table taking 3k inserts/sec.
constraints: 800M claim_lines, 3k inserts/sec
Existing 0.4% orphans must be cleaned before validation
Adding the FK must not block writes during validation
Postgres 13
Cleanup of orphans must be auditable, not silent
Rollback must not re-orphan rows

before_state.description: No FK; orphaned claim_lines exist and more can be created.

issues: No FK from claim_lines.claim_id to claims.id
0.4% of rows are orphaned after a bad bulk delete
New orphans can still be inserted
No referential check; a nonexistent claim_id is accepted

reasoning: Naive approach: ALTER TABLE ADD FOREIGN KEY in one statement — it scans all 800M rows to validate while holding a lock that blocks writes, and it outright fails on the existing orphans. Rejected alternative: enforce referential integrity only in the app — rejected because concurrent bulk jobs bypass the app and re-create orphans. Chosen path: quarantine and clean orphans auditable-y, add the FK as NOT VALID (cheap, immediately enforces new writes), then VALIDATE CONSTRAINT under a SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE lock that allows concurrent writes. Cross-layer trace: insert.js unchanged but now protected once FK NOT VALID is added -> data layer rejects bad claim_id immediately -> orphans moved to a quarantine table for audit -> VALIDATE runs without blocking inserts -> config: none -> tests assert new orphan insert fails and old orphans are quarantined.

after_state.description: Orphans quarantined; FK added NOT VALID to guard new writes immediately, then validated online.

files.purpose: Moves orphaned rows into an auditable quarantine table instead of silently deleting

files.content:
ALTER TABLE claim_lines
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_claim FOREIGN KEY (claim_id) REFERENCES claims(id) NOT VALID;
files.purpose: Immediately enforces referential integrity on new writes without scanning history

files.content: ALTER TABLE claim_lines VALIDATE CONSTRAINT fk_claim; -- SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, writes allowed
files.purpose: Validates existing rows online so the FK becomes fully trusted without a write block
Same code, now guarded by the database FK against orphan creation

migration_strategy: Quarantine existing orphans into claim_lines_orphans (auditable)
Add FK as NOT VALID so new inserts are immediately checked
Run VALIDATE CONSTRAINT online (writes continue under SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE)
Verify pg_constraint.convalidated is true; review quarantine table with ops
Set retention on quarantine table after sign-off
Rollback: DROP CONSTRAINT fk_claim; quarantined rows can be restored if needed
testing_strategy: Fixture: 5M claim_lines with 0.4% orphans across known claim ids. Assert output: after migration, count of orphans in claim_lines is zero and they all appear in claim_lines_orphans. Assert side-effect: an INSERT with a nonexistent claim_id raises foreign_key_violation once the NOT VALID FK is added. Chaos test: run a bulk insert of 10k lines (some with bad claim_ids) during VALIDATE and assert the bad ones are rejected and the valid ones commit without the VALIDATE blocking them.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A single-statement ADD FOREIGN KEY both fails on the existing orphans and, even after cleanup, scans 800M rows under a lock that blocks the 3k/sec insert stream.

title: Migrate timestamp columns from timestamp-without-tz to timestamptz across a schema
business_context: A global SaaS stored all timestamps as `timestamp without time zone` assuming server-local time. After moving servers from US-East to UTC infra, reports shifted by 4-5 hours and on-call misjudged incident timelines. They must convert 14 timestamp columns across 6 tables to timestamptz, interpreting old values as the original US/Eastern wall clock.
constraints: Old values were stored in America/New_York wall-clock implicitly
6 tables, 14 columns, largest table 400M rows
No double-applying the offset
Reads/writes continue during conversion
Postgres 14
Rollback must restore the original column type and values

before_state.description: timestamp-without-tz columns interpreted ambiguously after the infra timezone change.

issues: timestamp without time zone has no offset; meaning depends on server TZ
After moving to UTC infra the same values now read 4-5h off
Reports and incident timelines shifted
Client assumes occurred_at is UTC, but it was Eastern wall-clock

reasoning: Naive approach: ALTER COLUMN occurred_at TYPE timestamptz — Postgres interprets bare timestamps using the session TimeZone, which is now UTC, so all historical Eastern values get mislabeled as UTC, baking in the 4-5h error permanently. Rejected alternative: fix offsets in the application read layer — rejected because every one of dozens of readers would need the conversion and any miss yields inconsistent times. Chosen path: convert with an explicit AT TIME ZONE 'America/New_York' so old wall-clock values are correctly anchored, done column by column with a USING expression, in batches for large tables. Cross-layer trace: read.js can keep selecting -> conversion uses AT TIME ZONE to anchor history -> for the 400M-row table, add a new timestamptz column, backfill with the expression, then swap -> writes during conversion go to both via trigger -> tests assert a known Eastern value maps to the correct UTC instant.

after_state.description: Columns converted to timestamptz anchoring historical values to America/New_York; large tables migrated via add-backfill-swap.

files.content:
-- for small/medium tables: explicit anchor avoids the UTC mislabel
ALTER TABLE app_events
  ALTER COLUMN occurred_at TYPE timestamptz
  USING occurred_at AT TIME ZONE 'America/New_York';
files.purpose: Converts in place while interpreting stored wall-clock as Eastern, the original meaning
Migrates the 400M-row table online via add/backfill/swap to avoid a long rewrite lock

files.content:
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION set_ts_tz() RETURNS trigger AS $$
BEGIN NEW.ts_tz := COALESCE(NEW.ts_tz, NEW.ts AT TIME ZONE 'America/New_York'); RETURN NEW; END;$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;
files.purpose: Keeps the new tz column populated for writes during the large-table backfill
Reader unchanged; values are now unambiguous instants

migration_strategy: Convert small/medium tables in place with AT TIME ZONE 'America/New_York' USING clause
For the 400M-row table, add ts_tz, install sync trigger, backfill in id batches
Swap the large table column once backfill completes and parity is verified
Validate a set of known historical timestamps against expected UTC instants
Update client docs noting timestamps are now true instants
Rollback: keep original columns until sign-off; ALTER back TYPE timestamp USING ts AT TIME ZONE 'UTC' restores prior bytes
testing_strategy: Fixture: rows with known Eastern wall-clock values spanning a DST boundary (e.g., 2025-11-02 01:30). Assert output: converted timestamptz equals the correct UTC instant accounting for DST. Assert side-effect: on the large table, a write during backfill populates ts_tz via the trigger so no row is left NULL. Negative test: assert the naive in-place ALTER (without AT TIME ZONE) produces a 4-5h-off value, documenting why the explicit anchor is required.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A plain ALTER TYPE timestamptz reinterprets stored bare timestamps using the now-UTC session TimeZone, permanently baking a 4-5 hour error (and DST inconsistencies) into all historical data.

title: Adopt CDC via Debezium to replace dual-writes that drifted between Postgres and Elasticsearch
business_context: An e-commerce search index in Elasticsearch is kept in sync by application dual-writes after each product update. Under load, ES writes sometimes fail silently and the index drifts; 3% of products show stale prices. They must replace fragile dual-writes with Postgres-to-ES change data capture (Debezium) for exactly-once-ish, ordered updates.
constraints: 3% stale products from silent ES write failures
2k product updates/sec
Ordered updates per product (no out-of-order price overwrite)
ES reindex must be online; no search downtime
Postgres logical replication + Kafka Connect
Rollback to dual-write must be possible

before_state.description: App dual-writes to Postgres and ES; ES failures silently drift the index.

issues: ES failure is swallowed -> silent drift
No ordering guarantee between concurrent updates
No retry or backfill mechanism

reasoning: Naive approach: keep dual-writes but add a retry loop on ES failure — still loses ordering under concurrency and a crashed pod drops the in-flight ES write entirely. Rejected alternative: a nightly full reindex from Postgres — rejected because it leaves up to 24h of staleness and hammers ES with a full rebuild each night. Chosen path: capture Postgres row changes via Debezium logical decoding into Kafka, key messages by product id for per-key ordering, and a sink connector applies them to ES with offset-tracked at-least-once delivery plus idempotent upserts. Cross-layer trace: update.js writes only Postgres -> Debezium emits the change keyed by id -> Kafka preserves per-key order -> ES sink upserts idempotently using doc version -> initial snapshot backfills the index -> config: connector configs -> tests assert ES converges and ordering holds.

after_state.description: App writes only Postgres; Debezium streams ordered changes to Kafka; an ES sink applies idempotent, versioned upserts.

files.purpose: Removes the fragile dual-write; Postgres is the only write target
Captures product changes keyed by id (per-key ordering) and snapshots existing rows
Applies ordered, idempotent upserts/deletes to ES with offset-tracked delivery
Uses the row version as ES external version so a stale message can't overwrite newer data

migration_strategy: Stand up Debezium source with snapshot.mode=initial to backfill ES from Postgres
Deploy ES sink with UPSERT and external versioning; run alongside dual-write
Compare CDC-populated index vs dual-write index for a canary product set
Remove the app ES dual-write so Postgres is the single source
Decommission the old dual-write code path and add lag alerting on the connector
Rollback: re-enable dual-write; CDC pipeline can be paused without data loss
testing_strategy: Fixture: 500k products; fire 50k rapid price updates including two near-simultaneous updates to the same product. Assert output: ES price for every product equals Postgres after the stream drains. Assert side-effect: the ES doc _version equals products.version, and an out-of-order replay of an older change is rejected by external versioning. Chaos test: pause the ES sink for 60s during heavy writes, then resume, and assert ES catches up exactly with zero lost updates (offset replay).
what_breaks_naive_approach: Dual-write with retries still loses ordering under concurrent updates and drops in-flight ES writes when a pod crashes, so the index keeps drifting; CDC with per-key ordering and offset replay closes both gaps.

title: Split a wide hot table to reduce write amplification using vertical partitioning
business_context: A gaming backend's `players` table has 60 columns; a large `profile_json` (avg 14KB) and `last_position` (updated every 2s) sit in the same row. Every position update rewrites the whole 14KB row via TOAST churn, causing 5x write amplification and replica lag. They must vertically split hot, frequently-updated columns from cold, large ones.
constraints: last_position updated every 2s per active player (250k actives)
profile_json avg 14KB causes TOAST rewrite per update
Replica lag spikes to 12s from write amplification
Reads that need both must stay correct via a join/view
Postgres 14
Rollback to single table possible

before_state.description: Hot position updates rewrite a wide row containing a large cold JSON blob.

files.content:
CREATE TABLE players (
  id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,
  username TEXT,
  profile_json JSONB,        -- avg 14KB, rarely changes
  last_position POINT,        -- updated every 2s
  last_seen TIMESTAMPTZ,
  -- ...56 more columns
  level INT
);
issues: Updating last_position rewrites the whole row including 14KB profile_json
TOAST churn drives 5x write amplification
Replica lag spikes to 12s
Each move rewrites a wide row and re-TOASTs the JSON column

reasoning: Naive approach: store last_position in Redis only — loses durability and complicates the many SQL reads/joins that need authoritative position. Rejected alternative: move profile_json to a side table but keep everything else together — rejected because last_seen and other hot columns still share a wide row; the win is partial. Chosen path: vertically partition into players_core (stable identity + cold profile) and players_state (hot, frequently-updated columns) with a shared PK, expose a view for combined reads, and route hot updates only to players_state. Cross-layer trace: move.js updates players_state only -> small rows mean no TOAST churn -> reads needing both use a view joining on id -> backfill copies hot columns to players_state -> dual-write during transition keeps both consistent -> tests assert no write amplification and join correctness.

after_state.description: Hot columns live in a narrow players_state table; cold/large columns stay in players_core; a view recombines them for reads.

files.purpose: Isolates frequently-updated columns into a narrow table so updates don't rewrite the 14KB JSON
Preserves the original players shape for readers via a join view
Routes hot position writes to the narrow table, eliminating write amplification
Populates the state table from the pre-split snapshot idempotently

migration_strategy: Create players_state; backfill hot columns from the current table
Dual-write hot columns to both the original table and players_state
Drop hot columns from the renamed players_core and create the recombining view
Route move() writes to players_state; reads use the players view
Monitor replica lag and TOAST stats to confirm reduced amplification
Rollback: re-add columns to players_core from players_state and drop the view
testing_strategy: Fixture: 200k players each with a 14KB profile_json. Fire 100 position updates per player. Assert output: SELECT from the players view returns correct position and intact profile_json. Assert side-effect: pg_stat_user_tables shows updates hit players_state with no n_tup_upd on players_core's TOAST, confirming amplification is gone. Chaos test: kill the backfill mid-run and re-run; assert ON CONFLICT prevents duplicate state rows and the view stays consistent.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Keeping last_position in the wide row means every 2-second update re-TOASTs the 14KB profile_json, producing 5x write amplification and replica lag that a Redis-only or partial split does not fully resolve.

title: Migrate from offset pagination to keyset pagination for a deep-scroll feed
business_context: A social feed API uses LIMIT/OFFSET. Users scrolling past page 500 trigger OFFSET 25000 queries that scan and discard 25k rows, taking 4s and pinning CPU. With 90M posts and infinite scroll, deep pagination is the top DB hotspot. They must switch to keyset (cursor) pagination without breaking existing clients still sending page numbers.
constraints: OFFSET deep pages take 4s and pin CPU
Existing mobile clients send ?page=N; must keep working during rollout
Stable ordering required even as new posts arrive
90M posts, 8k feed requests/sec
Postgres 14
Rollback to offset must be safe

before_state.description: Offset pagination scans and discards rows for deep pages.

issues: OFFSET 25000 scans and discards 25k rows (4s)
Ordering shifts when new posts arrive, causing duplicates/skips
CPU pinned on deep scroll

reasoning: Naive approach: just cap the max page — breaks the product's infinite scroll and angers power users. Rejected alternative: cache full ordered id lists per user in Redis — rejected because the feed is global/personalized and the lists churn constantly, exploding cache memory. Chosen path: keyset pagination using (created_at, id) as a composite cursor with a supporting index, while keeping an offset compatibility shim for old clients that translates page to an approximate cursor for the first few pages only. Cross-layer trace: list.js accepts an opaque cursor -> WHERE (created_at,id) < (cursorTs,cursorId) ORDER BY ... LIMIT 20 uses the index -> old ?page clients hit a shim that serves early pages via offset and a cursor thereafter -> response returns nextCursor -> config flag enables keyset -> tests assert no duplicates/skips as new posts arrive.

after_state.description: Keyset pagination via a (created_at,id) composite cursor and index; an offset shim keeps legacy clients working.

files.content: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_posts_created_id ON posts(created_at DESC, id DESC);
files.purpose: Backs the keyset ordering so each page is an index range scan, not a discard scan
Implements keyset pagination with stable ordering and an opaque next cursor
Keeps legacy page-number clients working for shallow pages while steering deep scroll to cursors

files.content:
function encodeCursor(r){return Buffer.from(`${r.created_at.toISOString()}|${r.id}`).toString('base64');}
function decodeCursor(c){const [t,i]=Buffer.from(c,'base64').toString().split('|');return {ts:t,id:Number(i)};}
files.purpose: Encodes/decodes the composite cursor opaquely so clients don't depend on internals

migration_strategy: Build the (created_at DESC,id DESC) index CONCURRENTLY
Deploy keyset endpoint returning nextCursor; keep legacy ?page handler
Route new app versions to cursor pagination behind a feature flag
Cap legacy deep pages at page 5 and return Upgrade-Required for deeper scroll
Monitor p99 on deep scroll; confirm index range scans replace discard scans
Rollback: disable the flag; legacy offset handler still serves all pages
testing_strategy: Fixture: 1M posts with ties on created_at to exercise the id tiebreaker. Assert output: paging through the entire feed via cursors yields each post exactly once with no duplicates or skips. Assert side-effect: EXPLAIN shows an Index Scan range on idx_posts_created_id with no large Rows Removed by Filter. Chaos test: insert 1k new posts mid-pagination and assert the cursor stream neither duplicates already-seen posts nor skips, unlike the offset path which shifts.
what_breaks_naive_approach: OFFSET 25000 forces Postgres to read and discard 25,000 rows per deep page (4s, CPU-pinned), and inserts during scroll shift offsets so users see duplicate or skipped posts.

title: Convert a serial id to UUIDv7 to enable client-side id generation and merge-friendly sync
business_context: An offline-first field-service mobile app generates work orders on-device, but the server uses SERIAL ids, forcing a round-trip to assign ids and causing duplicate submissions when connectivity flaps. They must move `work_orders` to UUIDv7 so clients mint time-ordered ids locally, without breaking existing integer FKs from `attachments`.
constraints: Clients must mint ids offline; no server round-trip
UUIDv7 for time-ordered index locality
FK from attachments.work_order_id (INT) must migrate too
40M existing work orders
Postgres 15
Rollback must keep both id schemes resolvable

before_state.description: SERIAL ids require a server round-trip; offline clients can't pre-assign ids.

issues: SERIAL id assigned only by server -> offline clients can't create ids
Connectivity flaps cause duplicate submissions
Random UUIDv4 would hurt index locality; need UUIDv7
Server-assigned id forces a round-trip and breaks offline flows

reasoning: Naive approach: switch the PK to UUIDv4 generated server-side — doesn't solve offline creation and v4's randomness fragments the B-tree, hurting insert locality on 40M rows. Rejected alternative: keep SERIAL and dedupe duplicates server-side by a client request id — rejected because it still needs a round-trip to learn the final id and complicates client-side references. Chosen path: add a UUIDv7 column clients generate, dual-key during transition, migrate the FK to the UUID, and keep the legacy int resolvable via a mapping. Cross-layer trace: client mints uuid7 -> create.js inserts the provided uuid (idempotent on conflict) -> attachments gains work_order_uuid backfilled from the int FK via a mapping -> swap FK to UUID -> config: server accepts client id -> tests assert offline-minted id round-trips and FK integrity holds.

after_state.description: work_orders keyed by client-minted UUIDv7; attachments FK migrated to the UUID; legacy int retained for resolution.

files.purpose: Adds a unique UUID key to work_orders and a UUID FK column on attachments
Accepts client-minted UUIDv7 so creation needs no round-trip and retries are idempotent
Maps the legacy int FK to the new UUID FK in batches
Repoints the FK to the UUID online, keeping the int column as a fallback

migration_strategy: Add uuid column + unique constraint; backfill UUIDv7 values for existing orders
Ship client UUIDv7 generation and server-side ON CONFLICT idempotent insert
Add work_order_uuid to attachments and backfill from the int FK in batches
Add UUID FK as NOT VALID then VALIDATE online; keep int FK as fallback
Switch reads/joins to the UUID; later drop the int columns after grace period
Rollback: int id and FK remain functional; revert app to server-assigned ids
testing_strategy: Fixture: 500k work_orders + 1M attachments with int FKs. Assert output: an offline-minted UUIDv7 inserted twice (retry) yields exactly one row. Assert side-effect: after backfill, every attachment's work_order_uuid resolves to the same logical order as its int FK (join parity). Chaos test: simulate connectivity flap by submitting the same client UUID from two devices and assert ON CONFLICT prevents duplicates while both references resolve.
what_breaks_naive_approach: UUIDv4 server-side neither enables offline id minting nor preserves index locality; its randomness fragments the 40M-row B-tree and inflates write IO, while still requiring a round-trip.

title: Replace a slow LIKE search with a GIN trigram index built concurrently
business_context: A B2B contacts CRM supports `WHERE name ILIKE '%query%'` over 120M contacts. Each search does a sequential scan taking 6-9s and there is no index that helps leading-wildcard ILIKE. They must add Postgres pg_trgm GIN indexing online so substring search is sub-100ms without blocking the 1,500 contact inserts/sec.
constraints: 120M contacts; ILIKE '%q%' seq-scans for 6-9s
1.5k inserts/sec, no write downtime
Index build must be online and resumable
Postgres 14, pg_trgm available
Disk headroom 22% — GIN must fit
Rollback must not leave an invalid index used by the planner

before_state.description: Leading-wildcard ILIKE forces a sequential scan; no usable index.

issues: Leading wildcard defeats B-tree indexes -> sequential scan
6-9s latency on 120M rows
CPU pinned during searches
B-tree on name only helps prefix, not substring ILIKE
No trigram index

reasoning: Naive approach: CREATE INDEX ... gin(name gin_trgm_ops) without CONCURRENTLY — blocks writes for the whole 120M-row build (long). Rejected alternative: move search to Elasticsearch — rejected as overkill for this milestone given an existing Postgres index can hit the latency target without a new system to operate. Chosen path: enable pg_trgm and build a GIN trigram index CONCURRENTLY, then the existing ILIKE query uses it automatically. Cross-layer trace: search.js query unchanged -> planner uses the GIN trigram index for the substring match -> data layer returns matches via bitmap index scan -> no async needed -> config: ensure pg_trgm extension installed -> tests assert plan uses the GIN index and latency drops.

after_state.description: A concurrently-built GIN trigram index serves leading-wildcard ILIKE in sub-100ms; query unchanged.

files.purpose: Builds a trigram GIN index online so substring ILIKE uses an index scan
Same SQL, now backed by the trigram index for fast substring search

files.content:
