-- if a CONCURRENTLY build was interrupted, drop the invalid leftover then retry
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF EXISTS idx_contacts_name_trgm;
files.purpose: Cleans up an interrupted build so the planner never uses an invalid index

migration_strategy: Enable pg_trgm extension (idempotent)
Build the GIN trigram index CONCURRENTLY outside a transaction
If interrupted, DROP CONCURRENTLY the invalid index and rebuild
ANALYZE and verify EXPLAIN uses a bitmap index scan on a canary query
Monitor search p99 for 24h; tune work_mem if bitmap heap recheck is heavy
Rollback: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY; query reverts to seq scan
testing_strategy: Fixture: 3M contacts with realistic name distribution including shared substrings. Assert output: search('smith') returns the expected matching rows. Assert side-effect: EXPLAIN (ANALYZE) shows a Bitmap Index Scan on idx_contacts_name_trgm and execution under the latency budget, not a Seq Scan. Negative test: interrupt the CONCURRENTLY build, confirm the leftover index has indisvalid=false and is ignored, then drop and rebuild successfully.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A non-concurrent GIN build holds a lock that blocks all inserts on contacts for the entire 120M-row build, stalling the 1,500/sec write stream for an extended period.

title: Migrate a soft-delete flag column to row-level retention with partitioned archival
business_context: A SaaS document store uses `is_deleted` on a 500M-row `documents` table. 38% of rows are soft-deleted but never purged, so every query carries WHERE is_deleted=false and indexes are bloated with dead rows. They must move to a retention model that physically separates active from archived documents while preserving audit access for 7 years.
constraints: 38% soft-deleted rows bloat indexes and slow scans
7-year audit retention required for deleted docs
No query may accidentally surface deleted docs as active
4k document writes/sec
Postgres 14
Rollback must keep deleted docs queryable

before_state.description: Soft-delete flag bloats the hot table with dead rows that every query must filter.

issues: 38% dead rows inflate every index and scan
Every query must remember WHERE is_deleted=false
Indexes don't exclude deleted rows
A forgotten is_deleted filter leaks deleted docs
Index scans traverse deleted rows

reasoning: Naive approach: just DELETE soft-deleted rows — loses the 7-year audit trail required by compliance. Rejected alternative: add partial indexes WHERE is_deleted=false — rejected because it helps reads but the dead rows still bloat the heap and vacuum work, and a forgotten filter still leaks deletes. Chosen path: split into an active `documents` table and a partitioned `documents_archive` (by deleted year) for retention, move soft-deleted rows to the archive, and make delete an INSERT-into-archive + DELETE-from-active in one transaction. Cross-layer trace: list.js drops the is_deleted filter (active table has no deleted rows) -> delete moves the row to archive partition by year -> backfill migrates existing soft-deleted rows -> archive partitions enable cheap 7-year retention and detach -> tests assert deleted docs never appear in active reads.

after_state.description: Active and archived documents are physically separate; deletion moves rows to a year-partitioned archive; active reads need no filter.

files.purpose: Stores deleted documents in year partitions enabling cheap 7-year retention and detach
Atomically moves a document from active to the archive, keeping audit history
Removes the error-prone filter; deleted docs are physically absent from active reads

files.content:
WITH moved AS (
  DELETE FROM documents WHERE is_deleted=true
  RETURNING id,owner_id,body,deleted_at)
INSERT INTO documents_archive(id,owner_id,body,deleted_at)
SELECT id,owner_id,body,COALESCE(deleted_at,now()) FROM moved;
files.purpose: Migrates existing soft-deleted rows into the archive, shrinking the active table

migration_strategy: Create partitioned documents_archive with current-year partition
Deploy delete-as-move (active DELETE + archive INSERT) in one transaction
Backfill existing soft-deleted rows into the archive in batches
Drop is_deleted/deleted_at from active table and remove filters from queries
Schedule yearly partition creation and 7-year detach for retention
Rollback: keep is_deleted columns until sign-off; archive rows remain queryable
testing_strategy: Fixture: 1M documents with 38% soft-deleted. Assert output: listActive returns only live docs and archive holds exactly the deleted set. Assert side-effect: softDelete is atomic, the row exists in exactly one of active/archive (verify count(active)+count(archive)=total). Chaos test: kill the transaction between DELETE and INSERT (simulate crash) and assert the transaction rolls back so the doc stays active, never lost.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hard-deleting soft-deleted rows destroys the legally required 7-year audit trail, while partial indexes leave the dead rows bloating the heap and still allow a forgotten filter to leak deletes.

title: Migrate Kafka topic partition count for an orders stream without breaking key ordering
business_context: A payments platform's `orders` Kafka topic has 12 partitions and consumers can't keep up at 35k msg/sec; lag grows during peaks. Increasing partitions naively re-hashes keys so a given order's events scatter across partitions, breaking per-order ordering that the settlement consumer relies on. They must scale to 48 partitions while preserving per-order ordering.
constraints: 35k msg/sec, consumer lag during peaks
Per-order event ordering must be preserved
12 -> 48 partitions
Existing in-flight messages must not be reordered
Kafka with key-based partitioning
Rollback to 12 partitions must not lose messages

before_state.description: Default key hashing means adding partitions reshuffles existing keys, breaking ordering.

files.content: await producer.send({ topic: 'orders', messages: [{ key: orderId, value: JSON.stringify(evt) }] });
issues: Default partitioner = hash(key) % numPartitions
Changing numPartitions from 12 to 48 remaps every key
An order's events would split across old and new partitions
Relies on per-partition ordering for a given orderId
Breaks if the order's events scatter after repartitioning

reasoning: Naive approach: run kafka-topics --alter to increase partitions on the live topic — Kafka only adds empty partitions and the default partitioner immediately remaps keys, so an order whose past events sat on partition 3 now produces to partition 27, splitting its event stream and breaking ordering. Rejected alternative: a custom modulo partitioner that keeps key->partition stable — rejected because pinning keys to old partitions wastes the new partitions and creates skew. Chosen path: create a new topic `orders.v2` with 48 partitions and a sticky key partitioner, dual-produce, migrate consumers with offset translation, and drain the old topic in key order. Cross-layer trace: producer dual-writes to v1 and v2 -> v2 uses a deterministic murmur2 key partitioner so each order maps to exactly one v2 partition -> consumers switch to v2 after they fully drain v1 per partition -> a migration marker ensures no order is processed on both -> config flag selects topic -> tests assert per-order ordering on v2.

after_state.description: New 48-partition topic with deterministic key partitioning; dual-produce then consumer cutover preserving per-order ordering.

files.purpose: Dual-produces to old and new topics so v2 can be drained before cutover
Deterministically maps each orderId to exactly one v2 partition, preserving per-order ordering
Adds idempotency so the dual-produce window can't double-apply an order event

files.content:
1. Enable DUAL_PRODUCE=on. 2. Let v2 accumulate; consumers still on v1.
3. Per v1 partition: drain to end, record offsets. 4. Switch consumers to orders.v2.
5. Disable DUAL_PRODUCE. 6. Retire orders v1 after retention.
files.purpose: Documents the ordered drain-then-cutover so no order's events are processed out of order

migration_strategy: Create orders.v2 with 48 partitions and the deterministic key partitioner
Enable DUAL_PRODUCE so every event lands on both topics
Add consumer idempotency keyed by event id to tolerate the overlap
Drain v1 per-partition to its end, then switch consumers to orders.v2
Disable dual-produce; retire v1 after retention window
Rollback: re-enable v1 consumers (offsets retained); messages on v1 intact
testing_strategy: Fixture: 200k events across 5k orders, multiple events per order. Assert output: on orders.v2, all events for any given orderId land on a single partition (per-order ordering preserved). Assert side-effect: the settlement applied-set contains each event id exactly once despite dual-produce. Chaos test: kill a consumer mid-cutover and restart; assert idempotency prevents double-applied order events and offsets resume without reordering.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Altering partition count on the live topic remaps key->partition under the default hash, so an order's historical events stay on the old partition while new events go to a different one, splitting the stream and breaking the ordering settlement depends on.

title: Introduce read replicas with replication-lag-aware routing for read-after-write safety
business_context: A fintech dashboard moved heavy reads to a Postgres read replica to offload the primary, but users now see stale balances right after a transfer because the replica lags 200-800ms. They must route reads to replicas for throughput while guaranteeing read-after-write for the writer's own subsequent reads.
constraints: Replica lag 200-800ms under load
Read-after-write must hold for a user immediately after their write
60k reads/sec, 5k writes/sec
Multi-instance API (10 pods)
Postgres streaming replication, LSN available
Rollback to primary-only reads must be instant

before_state.description: All reads go to a replica; a user reads stale data right after writing.

issues: All reads use the replica unconditionally
No awareness of replication lag
User sees pre-write state after their own transfer
Reads from replica even immediately after a write to the same account

reasoning: Naive approach: send all reads to the replica — fast but violates read-after-write because the replica lags up to 800ms. Rejected alternative: sticky-route every request from a user to the primary for N seconds after any write — rejected because it floods the primary during active sessions and the N-second window is a guess that's both too long and sometimes too short. Chosen path: capture the primary's write LSN on each write, pass it forward (cookie/session), and route the user's next reads to a replica only if the replica has replayed past that LSN, else fall back to primary. Cross-layer trace: writePool returns the commit LSN -> the session stores last_write_lsn -> readPool checks replica's pg_last_wal_replay_lsn against last_write_lsn -> if replica caught up, read replica; else read primary -> config flag enables lag-aware routing -> tests assert no stale read after write.

after_state.description: LSN-aware routing: a user's reads use a replica only once it has replayed past their last write LSN, otherwise the primary.

files.purpose: Routes reads to a replica only when it has replayed past the caller's last write LSN
Carries the user's last write LSN across requests so routing decisions are per-user
Records the commit LSN after a transfer so subsequent reads can verify replica freshness
Selects the correct pool per request to guarantee read-after-write while still offloading reads

migration_strategy: Add writeReturningLsn and capture commit LSN on every write
Carry last_write_lsn per user via signed cookie/session (short TTL)
Route reads via readPoolFor: replica if caught up, else primary
Canary lag-aware routing for one cohort; measure stale-read rate (target 0)
Ramp to all pods; alert if primary read fraction spikes (replica too laggy)
Rollback: force readPoolFor to return primary for instant safety
testing_strategy: Fixture: primary + replica with an injected 500ms replay delay. Assert output: balance() immediately after transfer() returns the post-transfer balance (read-after-write). Assert side-effect: the read is served by the primary while the replica is behind the write LSN, and by the replica once it catches up (assert which pool answered). Chaos test: stall replica replay entirely and assert all post-write reads fall back to the primary with zero stale balances, then recover routing when replication resumes.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Unconditional replica reads return balances from before the user's just-committed transfer because the replica lags 200-800ms, showing wrong balances in a fintech dashboard.

title: Add list-partitioning by region to enforce data residency for a multinational SaaS
business_context: A B2B SaaS must comply with EU data residency: EU tenant rows must physically live on EU storage. All tenants currently share one `accounts` table on US infra. They need LIST partitioning by region with EU partitions on an EU tablespace, migrated without downtime for 90M accounts.
constraints: EU rows must reside on EU-located tablespace
90M accounts, 4k writes/sec
No cross-region query may pull EU PII into US logs
Region derived from tenant config, immutable per tenant
Postgres 15 with tablespaces per region
Rollback must not strand EU data in the wrong region

before_state.description: Single US table holds all regions; no residency enforcement.

issues: EU rows stored on US infra, violating residency
No partitioning by region
PII can land in US backups/logs
No routing of EU writes to EU storage

reasoning: Naive approach: keep one table and just filter by region in queries — does nothing for physical residency; EU PII still sits on US disks and in US backups, failing the legal requirement. Rejected alternative: a separate EU database with cross-DB federation — rejected because cross-database joins and 2-phase writes add operational complexity beyond what LIST partitioning with tablespaces solves. Chosen path: LIST-partition accounts by region with EU partitions pinned to an EU tablespace, dual-write through the partitioned parent, backfill EU rows into the EU partition, and ensure the PK includes region. Cross-layer trace: write.js inserts through the partitioned parent which routes by region -> EU rows land on EU tablespace physically -> PK becomes (id, region) for partitioning -> backfill copies EU rows to EU partition -> logging redaction ensures US logs never carry EU PII -> tests assert EU rows on EU tablespace.

after_state.description: accounts LIST-partitioned by region; EU partition on an EU tablespace; writes route automatically.

files.purpose: Pins EU rows to an EU tablespace via list partitioning, enforcing physical residency
Writes through the partitioned parent so residency routing is transparent
Backfills rows into region partitions, placing EU data physically in the EU tablespace
Prevents EU PII from leaking into US-hosted logs, complementing physical residency

migration_strategy: Create EU/US tablespaces and the LIST-partitioned parent
Dual-write through accounts_p alongside the legacy table
Backfill EU then US rows into their partitions on the correct tablespaces
Cut reads to the partitioned parent via a view; verify EU rows on ts_eu
Decommission legacy table after residency audit passes
Rollback: legacy table retains all rows; repoint reads while EU partition stays put
testing_strategy: Fixture: 1M accounts split EU/US. Assert output: reads via the parent return correct rows per region. Assert side-effect: query pg_class/pg_tablespace to confirm every EU row's partition resides on ts_eu and no EU row sits on ts_us. Negative test: attempt to insert region='EU' and assert it physically lands in accounts_eu (relfilenode on EU tablespace), and assert logAccount never emits EU pii fields.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Filtering by region in queries leaves EU PII physically on US disks and in US backups, which fails the data-residency requirement regardless of how reads are filtered.

title: Replace a global sequence bottleneck with cached sequence ranges per pod
business_context: An ad-tech ingest service inserts 90k impression rows/sec. The single Postgres sequence backing the PK serializes nextval across all 12 pods, and the sequence's WAL flushes became a contention point limiting throughput. They must remove the per-row sequence round-trip without losing uniqueness.
constraints: 90k inserts/sec across 12 pods
PK must stay globally unique and monotonic-ish for index locality
No central coordinator round-trip per row
Postgres 14
Gaps in ids are acceptable; duplicates are not
Rollback to the global sequence must be safe

before_state.description: A single sequence serializes id generation across all pods.

issues: Single sequence nextval serializes across 12 pods
Sequence WAL flush is a contention hotspot
Per-row round-trip to fetch nextval
Relies on DEFAULT nextval -> sequence contention per insert

reasoning: Naive approach: bump the sequence CACHE to a large number — helps a little but cache is per-session, so connection pool churn still hits the sequence often and ordering across pods stays serialized at the WAL. Rejected alternative: use a UUID PK to avoid sequences — rejected because random UUIDs fragment the B-tree at 90k/sec, hurting insert locality and bloating WAL. Chosen path: allocate large id ranges to each pod from a control table in one transaction, then mint ids locally from the in-memory range, refilling when low. Cross-layer trace: insert.js asks the local allocator for an id -> allocator hands out from its reserved block -> when the block is ~80% used, it reserves the next block via a single UPDATE...RETURNING on a ranges table -> ids are unique because blocks never overlap -> config: block size -> tests assert no duplicate ids across pods.

after_state.description: Each pod reserves large id blocks atomically and mints ids in-memory, eliminating per-row sequence contention.

files.purpose: Tracks the next unallocated block so pods can reserve disjoint ranges
Mints ids from a locally reserved block and refills via a single atomic UPDATE when low
Inserts with a locally minted id, removing the per-row sequence round-trip

files.content:
-- start allocator above the current sequence max to avoid collision
UPDATE id_allocator SET next_block = (SELECT (last_value / 100000) + 2 FROM impressions_id_seq) WHERE name='impressions';
files.purpose: Seeds the allocator past existing ids so legacy and new ids never collide

migration_strategy: Create id_allocator and seed next_block above the current sequence high-water mark
Deploy the local allocator and switch inserts to app-minted ids
Canary one pod; verify no id collisions against the legacy sequence
Ramp all pods; the sequence is no longer used for new rows
Drop the DEFAULT nextval on the column after confirming all writers use the allocator
Rollback: re-enable DEFAULT nextval; allocator blocks are disjoint so no conflict
testing_strategy: Fixture: 12 simulated pods each minting 1M ids concurrently. Assert output: the union of all ids has zero duplicates. Assert side-effect: id_allocator.next_block advanced exactly once per reserved block (no lost or double-reserved blocks). Chaos test: kill a pod mid-block; assert its unused ids are simply skipped (gaps allowed) and no other pod ever receives an overlapping id.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A single sequence serializes nextval and WAL-flushes per allocation across all 12 pods, capping throughput; merely enlarging CACHE doesn't remove the cross-pod serialization, and UUIDs fragment the index.

title: Migrate from a shared schema multi-tenant model to schema-per-tenant for noisy-neighbor isolation
business_context: A B2B analytics SaaS uses a shared `events` table with a tenant_id column. A few enterprise tenants with 500M+ rows each cause table bloat and vacuum storms that slow every tenant. They must migrate to schema-per-tenant so large tenants get isolated tables, indexes, and vacuum, without downtime for 4,000 tenants.
constraints: Noisy enterprise tenants degrade shared-table performance
4,000 tenants; largest has 500M rows
Per-tenant isolation of vacuum and indexes
Connection-level search_path routing
Postgres 14
Rollback to shared schema must be possible per tenant

before_state.description: All tenants share one events table; large tenants cause global vacuum storms.

issues: Enterprise tenants bloat the shared heap and indexes
Global vacuum can't isolate a single tenant
Every query filters tenant_id, scanning a giant shared index
All tenants contend on one table's locks and vacuum

reasoning: Naive approach: partition the shared table by tenant_id hash — helps vacuum somewhat but a 500M-row tenant still shares a partition with others and per-tenant index tuning is impossible. Rejected alternative: a separate database per tenant — rejected because 4,000 databases overwhelm connection management and migrations. Chosen path: schema-per-tenant within one database, route via search_path per connection, migrate large/noisy tenants first by copying their rows into their own schema, and keep small tenants shared until ready. Cross-layer trace: repo.js sets search_path to the tenant schema so unqualified `events` resolves per-tenant -> data layer gives each big tenant its own table/indexes/vacuum -> backfill copies a tenant's rows into its schema -> a routing map records which tenants are migrated -> tests assert isolation and correct routing.

after_state.description: Each migrated tenant gets a dedicated schema with its own events table; connection search_path routes queries; small tenants stay shared.

files.purpose: Gives a tenant an isolated table so its vacuum and indexes don't affect others
Routes a tenant's query to its schema via search_path, isolating large tenants
Copies a tenant's rows into its schema then removes them from the shared table

files.content:
const migrated = new Set(); // loaded from a routing table
async function tenantSchema(tenantId) { return migrated.has(tenantId) ? `tenant_${tenantId}` : 'public'; }
files.purpose: Tracks which tenants are migrated so routing is correct during the rollout

migration_strategy: Create a routing table; migrate the noisiest enterprise tenants first into their own schemas
Dual-write a tenant's events to both shared and its schema during its cutover
Verify row parity, then flip the tenant's routing flag and stop shared writes
Delete the tenant's rows from the shared table in batches to reclaim space
Repeat per tenant; small tenants may remain shared indefinitely
Rollback per tenant: flip routing back to public; shared rows retained until delete step
testing_strategy: Fixture: shared events with one 5M-row enterprise tenant and 50 small tenants. Assert output: recent(42) returns the same rows from its schema as it did from the shared table. Assert side-effect: after migration, vacuuming tenant_42.events does not touch public.events (verify via pg_stat). Chaos test: kill migration mid-copy and re-run; assert ON CONFLICT avoids duplicates and the routing flag stays off until parity is confirmed, so reads still hit the shared table safely.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hash-partitioning the shared table still co-locates a 500M-row tenant with others in a partition and prevents per-tenant index/vacuum tuning, so noisy-neighbor vacuum storms persist.

title: Rebuild a bloated index with zero downtime using REINDEX CONCURRENTLY
business_context: A logistics tracking service has a primary-key index on `parcel_scans` that grew to 3x its data size after years of churn; index-only scans degraded and disk is 88% full. The team must rebuild the bloated index online. They previously tried plain REINDEX and it locked writes on the 700M-row table for 25 minutes.
constraints: 700M rows, 2k scan inserts/sec
Disk at 88% — rebuild must not double permanent space
No write downtime
Postgres 12+ (REINDEX CONCURRENTLY available)
Must handle a failed concurrent rebuild leaving an invalid index
Rollback must keep a valid usable index

before_state.description: A 3x-bloated index degrades scans and threatens disk; plain REINDEX locks writes.

files.content: REINDEX TABLE parcel_scans; -- locks the table, blocked writes 25 min
issues: Plain REINDEX takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE, blocking writes
25-minute write outage on 700M rows
Bloat: index 3x heap size, 88% disk
Index-only scans degraded by bloat

reasoning: Naive approach: REINDEX TABLE during a quiet hour — still takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE and blocked writes for 25 minutes, unacceptable for a 24/7 tracking service. Rejected alternative: pg_repack the whole table — rejected because repack needs roughly a full table-size of extra disk and we're at 88%, risking a full disk. Chosen path: REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY which builds a new index alongside, swaps, and drops the old, needing only one index's worth of extra space, with explicit handling for the INVALID-index failure case. Cross-layer trace: lookup.js unchanged -> REINDEX CONCURRENTLY builds a fresh index then atomically swaps -> only transient extra space equal to the rebuilt index -> on failure an _ccnew invalid index remains and must be dropped -> config: none -> tests assert writes continue and index size shrinks.

after_state.description: Index rebuilt online via REINDEX CONCURRENTLY with explicit failure cleanup; writes never blocked.

files.content:
-- online rebuild; only one index's worth of transient extra space
REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY parcel_scans_pkey;
files.purpose: Rebuilds the bloated index without an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock on the table
Detects and drops a leftover invalid index from an interrupted rebuild
Same query, now served by the de-bloated index with restored index-only scans

migration_strategy: Pre-check disk has room for one rebuilt index (not the whole table)
Run REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY on the bloated index outside a transaction
If it fails, query pg_index for indisvalid=false and DROP CONCURRENTLY the leftover
Verify new index size and ANALYZE; confirm index-only scans restored
Schedule periodic bloat monitoring to avoid recurrence
Rollback: the old index remains valid until the concurrent swap completes; abort is safe
testing_strategy: Fixture: 5M-row table with an artificially bloated index. Assert output: byId returns correct rows throughout the rebuild. Assert side-effect: post-rebuild index size (pg_relation_size) is markedly smaller and writes during the rebuild all committed (insert a stream during REINDEX and verify counts). Chaos test: kill the REINDEX CONCURRENTLY session mid-build and assert an invalid _ccnew index appears, the table still has a valid usable index, and the cleanup drops the invalid one.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Plain REINDEX takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE and blocks all writes for the full 25-minute rebuild, and pg_repack needs roughly the whole table's size in free space which the 88%-full disk can't provide.

title: Migrate a counter from per-row UPDATE to an append-only ledger with periodic rollup
business_context: A gaming leaderboard increments `scores.points` on every play event (120k/sec). The single-row hot UPDATE per player causes lock contention and dead-tuple bloat, and top players' rows become serialization bottlenecks. They must switch to an append-only points ledger with a periodic rollup to a materialized total.
constraints: 120k score increments/sec; hot-row UPDATE contention
Top-player rows serialize under contention
Leaderboard reads must stay fast
Total must be eventually exact
Postgres 14
Rollback to direct UPDATE must be safe

before_state.description: Every play event does a hot single-row UPDATE, contending on top players.

issues: Hot-row UPDATE serializes concurrent increments on top players
Each UPDATE creates a dead tuple -> heavy vacuum
Lock contention caps throughput
Reads a single row that's constantly being updated and locked

reasoning: Naive approach: shard the counter into N sub-rows per player and sum them — reduces contention but complicates reads and still does in-place UPDATEs that bloat. Rejected alternative: move the counter to Redis INCR — rejected because durability and exact reconciliation with the authoritative leaderboard become hard and a Redis loss drops points. Chosen path: append increments to an INSERT-only ledger (no row contention), maintain a rolled-up `score_totals` updated periodically, and serve reads as rollup + un-rolled ledger delta. Cross-layer trace: add.js INSERTs a ledger row (no lock contention) -> reads return score_totals.total plus the sum of ledger rows since last rollup -> a rollup job periodically folds ledger rows into the total and advances a watermark -> config: rollup interval -> tests assert total equals sum of all increments.

after_state.description: Increments are append-only ledger inserts; a rollup job folds them into a total; reads combine the rollup with the un-rolled delta.

files.purpose: Stores increments append-only and a rolled-up total with a watermark
Eliminates hot-row UPDATE contention by appending increments
Returns the exact total by combining the rollup with un-rolled ledger increments
Periodically folds new ledger rows into the total and advances the watermark idempotently

migration_strategy: Create score_ledger and score_totals; seed totals from current scores.points
Deploy append-only addPoints alongside a read that combines rollup + delta
Run the rollup job on an interval; verify totals converge to sums
Cut reads fully to the combined read; stop updating scores.points
Add retention to prune rolled-up ledger rows older than the watermark
Rollback: revert addPoints to UPDATE scores.points (seeded from totals)
testing_strategy: Fixture: 10k players, fire 1M random increments. Assert output: total(player) equals the exact sum of that player's increments at any point (before and after rollup). Assert side-effect: rollup advances last_rolled_id and never double-counts (run rollup twice, total unchanged). Chaos test: run 200 concurrent increments to one top player and assert zero lock-wait timeouts (append has no row contention) and the final total is exact.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Per-row UPDATEs serialize all concurrent increments to a top player's single row and generate dead tuples that bloat the table, capping throughput far below 120k/sec.

title: Migrate from MongoDB to Postgres for transactional integrity in an order pipeline
business_context: A marketplace ran orders and payments in MongoDB, but multi-document updates without transactions caused orders marked paid while inventory wasn't decremented during partial failures. With 60M orders they must move the transactional core (orders, payments, inventory) to Postgres for ACID guarantees while keeping the MongoDB read API alive during migration.
constraints: Multi-doc partial failures left inconsistent state
60M orders to migrate online
Mongo read API must keep serving during migration
Strict consistency: paid implies inventory decremented
Dual-write must not diverge
Rollback to Mongo must be possible mid-migration

before_state.description: Mongo multi-document order/payment updates lack atomicity, causing inconsistent partial states.

issues: Two updates are not atomic; a crash between them leaves PAID with stock not decremented
No transaction across collections
Inconsistent state requires manual reconciliation

reasoning: Naive approach: enable MongoDB multi-document transactions and stay on Mongo — transactions exist but the team also needs relational constraints (FK, CHECK) and reporting joins that Postgres serves better, and the migration is already mandated. Rejected alternative: big-bang export/import during a maintenance window — rejected because 60M orders take too long and the read API can't go dark. Chosen path: dual-write the transactional path to Postgres in a single ACID transaction, keep Mongo as the read source until backfill completes, then cut reads over. Cross-layer trace: pay.js writes order status + inventory decrement in one Postgres transaction (atomic) and mirrors to Mongo during transition -> backfill copies historical orders into Postgres idempotently -> read API switches to Postgres behind a flag -> a reconciliation audit compares the two stores -> tests assert paid-implies-decremented invariant.

after_state.description: Transactional core in Postgres with atomic pay+decrement; Mongo mirrored during transition; reads cut over after backfill.

files.content:
CREATE TABLE orders (id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, status TEXT NOT NULL);
CREATE TABLE inventory (sku TEXT PRIMARY KEY, stock INT NOT NULL CHECK (stock >= 0));
files.purpose: Defines the relational core with a CHECK that enforces non-negative stock
Makes pay+decrement atomic so PAID can never persist without a stock decrement
Idempotently copies historical orders from Mongo into Postgres
Routes reads to Postgres post-backfill with a flag to fall back to Mongo

migration_strategy: Stand up Postgres schema with constraints; enable atomic pay+decrement transaction
Mirror writes to Mongo during transition so the Mongo read API stays current
Backfill historical orders into Postgres idempotently via upsert
Run a reconciliation audit comparing Postgres vs Mongo order/inventory states
Flip ORDER_READ to pg; keep Mongo mirror for one cycle as fallback
Rollback: set ORDER_READ=mongo; Mongo mirror has consistent state
testing_strategy: Fixture: 100k orders in Mongo plus a low-stock SKU. Assert output: getOrder returns the same status from Postgres as Mongo post-backfill. Assert side-effect: a pay() that fails the stock check leaves orders.status unchanged (transaction rolled back) — the paid-implies-decremented invariant holds. Chaos test: crash the process between the two Postgres updates by forcing an error after the orders UPDATE and assert the whole transaction rolls back, unlike the Mongo version which left PAID with full stock.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Without a cross-collection transaction, a crash after marking the order PAID but before decrementing inventory leaves an oversold, paid order that needs manual reconciliation.

title: Add a covering index and drop a redundant one to fix write-amplifying over-indexing
business_context: A fintech ledger table has 9 indexes accumulated over years; inserts at 8k/sec are slow because every insert updates all 9 indexes, and 3 of them are never used by the planner. They must remove dead indexes and consolidate two overlapping ones into a single covering index to cut write amplification without breaking any query plan.
constraints: 8k inserts/sec; each insert maintains 9 indexes
3 indexes have zero scans in pg_stat_user_indexes over 30 days
Two indexes overlap on a prefix; consolidate safely
No query plan may regress
Postgres 14
Rollback must restore dropped indexes quickly

before_state.description: Over-indexed table; unused and overlapping indexes amplify every write.

issues: 9 indexes maintained per insert -> write amplification
idx_l_acct is a prefix of idx_l_acct_date (redundant)
3 indexes unused for 30 days
Each insert updates all 9 indexes

reasoning: Naive approach: drop the three zero-scan indexes immediately — mostly right, but dropping without CONCURRENTLY takes a brief exclusive lock and risks removing one that a rare monthly report needs. Rejected alternative: leave indexes and just add hardware — rejected because IOPS scaling masks the real cost and the redundant prefix index still wastes writes. Chosen path: verify zero usage over a full reporting cycle, drop unused indexes CONCURRENTLY, and replace the overlapping pair with a single covering index that serves both query shapes. Cross-layer trace: insert.js unchanged but now maintains fewer indexes -> the covering index serves both account-only and account+date queries -> drop redundant prefix index -> drop confirmed-unused indexes CONCURRENTLY -> config: monitor plans -> tests assert no plan regression.

after_state.description: Unused indexes dropped concurrently; overlapping pair consolidated into one covering index, cutting per-insert index maintenance.

files.content:
-- covering index serves both account_id and (account_id, created_at) queries
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_l_acct_date_amt ON ledger(account_id, created_at) INCLUDE (amount_cents);
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_l_acct;        -- prefix of the new index
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_l_acct_date;   -- superseded by the covering index
files.purpose: Replaces two overlapping indexes with one covering index that satisfies both query shapes
Removes confirmed-unused indexes to cut write amplification

files.content:
SELECT relname, indexrelname, idx_scan FROM pg_stat_user_indexes
WHERE relname='ledger' ORDER BY idx_scan;
files.purpose: Provides the usage evidence required before dropping any index

migration_strategy: Capture pg_stat_user_indexes idx_scan over a full month-end cycle to confirm zero usage
Create the covering index CONCURRENTLY; verify it serves both query shapes via EXPLAIN
Drop the two overlapping indexes CONCURRENTLY
Drop the three confirmed-unused indexes CONCURRENTLY
Measure insert latency and write IO reduction over 48h
Rollback: recreate any dropped index CONCURRENTLY from the saved DDL
testing_strategy: Fixture: 5M ledger rows and the representative account/date queries plus the rare report query. Assert output: every representative query returns identical rows after consolidation. Assert side-effect: EXPLAIN confirms the covering index is used for both account-only and account+date queries (no Seq Scan), and insert latency drops measurably with fewer indexes. Negative test: run the rare monthly report and assert it still has an index to use (proving we didn't drop one it needs) before finalizing.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Dropping indexes without first confirming zero usage across a full reporting cycle can remove an index a rare monthly report depends on, causing a sudden seq-scan regression at month-end.

title: Migrate a star-schema fact table to columnar storage for analytics scans
business_context: A B2B analytics product runs aggregate scans over a 4B-row `fact_usage` table in row-store Postgres; full-table aggregations take 90s and saturate IO. They must move the fact table to columnar storage (Citus columnar / cstore) to cut scan IO 10x, while OLTP-style point lookups by primary key remain on the row store.
constraints: 4B rows; aggregate scans take 90s
Point lookups by id must stay fast (row store)
Daily loads of 40M rows must remain fast
No downtime for the analytics API
Postgres 14 + columnar access method
Rollback to row store must be possible

before_state.description: Row-store fact table makes wide aggregate scans IO-bound.

files.content:
CREATE TABLE fact_usage (
  id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,
  tenant_id BIGINT,
  day DATE,
  metric TEXT,
  value BIGINT
);
issues: Row store reads all columns even for single-column aggregates
Full-table SUM scans 4B wide rows (90s)
IO saturated during analytics
Aggregation reads full rows; high IO and CPU

reasoning: Naive approach: convert fact_usage in place to columnar — columnar tables don't support efficient single-row UPDATE/point-DELETE, breaking any OLTP access and indexes the id lookups rely on. Rejected alternative: move everything to a separate columnar warehouse (e.g., ClickHouse) — rejected as too large a step for this milestone; staying in Postgres with a columnar AM meets the IO goal. Chosen path: keep recent/hot partitions row-store for point lookups and load older, immutable partitions into columnar, using partitioning to route by age and a UNION view for queries spanning both. Cross-layer trace: agg.js queries the partitioned parent -> old partitions are columnar (10x less IO) -> hot partition stays row-store for point lookups by id -> daily load appends to the row-store hot partition then later compacts to columnar -> config: age threshold -> tests assert aggregate IO drops and point lookups still work.

after_state.description: Partitioned fact table with hot row-store partitions for lookups and columnar partitions for historical aggregate scans.

files.purpose: Keeps the current month row-store so id point lookups stay fast
Stores immutable historical data columnar to cut aggregate scan IO ~10x
Queries the partitioned parent so columnar partitions serve historical aggregates
Compacts an aged partition into columnar storage via build-and-swap

migration_strategy: Create the partitioned parent; route writes to the row-store hot partition
Backfill historical months into columnar partitions via build-and-swap
Switch analytics reads to the partitioned parent (pruning + columnar scans)
Schedule monthly compaction of the aged row partition to columnar
Verify point lookups by id still hit the row-store hot partition's index
Rollback: detach columnar partitions and reattach row-store copies
testing_strategy: Fixture: 50M rows across 3 months (current row-store, older columnar). Assert output: monthly() returns identical aggregates from row-store and columnar partitions. Assert side-effect: EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) shows far fewer buffers read for columnar partitions versus row-store on the same aggregate. Negative test: assert a point lookup by id on a columnar historical partition still returns the row (correctness), while confirming the hot-month lookups use the row-store index.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Converting the whole fact table to columnar in place breaks point lookups and per-row updates the OLTP path depends on, since columnar storage isn't designed for single-row mutation.

title: Introduce row-level security to enforce tenant isolation during a shared-table migration
business_context: A healthcare SaaS shares a `records` table across clinics with tenant_id filtering done in application code. A bug omitted the filter in one endpoint and leaked 220 patient records cross-tenant. They must enforce tenant isolation at the database level via row-level security without breaking existing queries or admin reporting.
constraints: Tenant filter currently in app code; one bug leaked cross-tenant data
RLS must not break admin/reporting that spans tenants
Connection sets tenant context per request
3k queries/sec
Postgres 14
Rollback must restore unrestricted (app-filtered) access

before_state.description: Tenant isolation enforced only in app code; a missed filter leaks data.

issues: search() forgot the tenant_id filter -> cross-tenant leak
Isolation depends on every query remembering the filter
No DB-level guarantee

reasoning: Naive approach: add a code review checklist requiring the tenant filter — humans miss it; the leak already happened despite reviews. Rejected alternative: a per-tenant database — rejected as too heavy for thousands of small clinics on this timeline. Chosen path: enable Postgres row-level security with a policy keyed to a per-connection `app.tenant_id` setting, set that GUC at the start of each request, and grant a BYPASSRLS role only to vetted reporting jobs. Cross-layer trace: each request sets SET app.tenant_id from the authenticated session -> RLS policy restricts every query to that tenant automatically, even the buggy search() -> repo.js can drop redundant WHERE clauses -> reporting uses a BYPASSRLS role explicitly -> config: enable RLS gradually behind a flag -> tests assert the buggy query returns only the tenant's rows.

after_state.description: RLS policy scopes every query to the connection's tenant; reporting uses an explicit bypass role; isolation no longer depends on app filters.

files.purpose: Enforces tenant scoping at the database for every query on records
Sets and resets the per-request tenant context that RLS reads
Queries are now safe-by-default; the previously-buggy search is contained by RLS
Performs sanctioned cross-tenant reporting via an explicit bypass role, not by accident

migration_strategy: Add context middleware that sets app.tenant_id per authenticated request
Enable RLS and the policy on records behind a flag in staging first
Verify all endpoints still return correct per-tenant results (RLS scoping)
Grant BYPASSRLS only to the audited reporting role and migrate reports to it
Enable RLS in production; monitor for permission-denied anomalies
Rollback: DISABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; app-level filters remain as before
testing_strategy: Fixture: records for tenants A and B with overlapping note text. Assert output: list(A) returns only A's rows. Assert side-effect: the previously-buggy search() with no tenant filter returns only the current connection's tenant rows (RLS contained the leak). Negative test: run search() as tenant A for a term that matches tenant B's records and assert zero B rows are returned, then run the reporting role and assert it can see both tenants intentionally.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Relying on app code to add the tenant filter fails the moment any query omits it, as the real incident showed; RLS makes isolation a database invariant independent of code correctness.

title: Migrate a many-to-many join table to include a soft-versioned audit history
business_context: A B2B SaaS tracks user-to-role assignments in a simple `user_roles` join table. Compliance now requires a full audit of who had which role and when, including past assignments. The current table only stores current state; assigning/revoking loses history. They must add temporal versioning to 40M assignments without breaking permission checks that run 50k/sec.
constraints: Permission checks run 50k/sec and must stay fast (current-state lookup)
Full history of assignments/revocations required for audit
40M current assignments to migrate
No double-counting a role during the transition
Postgres 14
Rollback must keep current-state checks working

before_state.description: Join table holds only current state; revoking a role erases history.

issues: No history: a DELETE on revoke loses the audit trail
No granted_at/revoked_at timestamps
Compliance can't answer 'who had role X last March'
Fast current-state check but no temporal awareness

reasoning: Naive approach: add granted_at/revoked_at to the same table and never delete — current-state checks now must filter WHERE revoked_at IS NULL on every 50k/sec query, and the table bloats with revoked rows hurting the hot lookup. Rejected alternative: store history in an event log only and rebuild current state by replay — rejected because permission checks can't afford a replay per request. Chosen path: keep a lean current-state `user_roles` for fast checks and add an append-only `user_role_history` written transactionally on every grant/revoke. Cross-layer trace: check.js still hits the lean current-state table (fast) -> grant/revoke writes both the current-state change and a history row in one transaction -> backfill seeds history with current assignments as granted events -> audit queries hit the history table -> config: none -> tests assert current check is unaffected and history is complete.

after_state.description: Lean current-state table preserved for fast checks; an append-only history table records every grant/revoke transactionally.

files.purpose: Stores the full temporal audit trail without affecting the hot current-state lookup
Mutates current state and appends a history event atomically so the audit can't drift
Keeps permission checks fast by leaving the current-state table lean
Seeds the history with current assignments as GRANT events, idempotently

migration_strategy: Create user_role_history; backfill current assignments as GRANT events
Deploy grant/revoke to write current state + history in one transaction
Verify audit queries reconstruct point-in-time role membership correctly
Confirm hasRole latency unchanged (still hits the lean table)
Add retention/archival policy for very old history if needed
Rollback: stop writing history; current-state table and checks unaffected
testing_strategy: Fixture: 100k current assignments. Assert output: hasRole returns the same results as before with no latency change. Assert side-effect: each grant/revoke writes exactly one history row in the same transaction as the current-state change (atomicity). Chaos test: force an error after the current-state change but before the history insert and assert the transaction rolls back so current state and history never diverge.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Storing history in the same table forces every 50k/sec permission check to filter out revoked rows and lets revoked rows bloat the hot index, degrading the latency-critical lookup.

title: Migrate a hot-key Redis counter to sharded keys to beat single-shard CPU limits
business_context: A live-streaming platform tracks concurrent viewers per stream in Redis with INCR/DECR on one key per stream. A viral stream's single key saturated one Redis cluster shard's CPU (cross-slot hot key), causing latency for all keys on that shard. They must shard the hot counter across N keys mapped to different slots without losing count accuracy.
constraints: One viral stream's key saturates a single cluster shard
Counter must read accurately (sum within ~1s)
200k INCR/DECR per second on hot streams
Redis Cluster with hash slots
No central lock per increment
Rollback to single key must be safe

before_state.description: Single counter key per stream creates a cross-slot hot key on one shard.

issues: Single key -> all writes hit one slot/shard
Viral stream saturates that shard's CPU
Latency spillover to unrelated keys on the shard

reasoning: Naive approach: add a per-instance in-memory counter and flush periodically — drifts under crashes and makes current() approximate without a clear bound. Rejected alternative: move the counter to a database with atomic increments — rejected because the DB can't sustain 200k inc/sec on a single row any better than one Redis key. Chosen path: shard each stream's counter into K sub-keys using a hash-tag-free suffix so sub-keys distribute across slots, increment a randomly chosen shard, and read by summing all K shards. Cross-layer trace: join/leave pick a shard suffix (random) and INCR that sub-key -> sub-keys land on different cluster slots, spreading CPU -> current() reads all K sub-keys (MGET via per-slot batching) and sums -> K chosen to spread but keep reads cheap -> config: K per stream tier -> tests assert summed count equals number of joins minus leaves.

after_state.description: Each stream counter is sharded into K sub-keys across slots; increments target a random shard; reads sum all shards.

files.purpose: Spreads increments across K slot-distributed sub-keys and reads the total by summing
Folds the existing single-key count into a shard so no viewers are lost at cutover

files.content:
// hot streams use more shards; small streams use 1 to avoid read fan-out
module.exports = (tier) => (tier === 'viral' ? 16 : tier === 'big' ? 4 : 1);
files.purpose: Tunes shard count per stream tier to balance write spread against read cost

migration_strategy: Deploy sharded counter behind a flag; new joins/leaves use sub-keys
Migrate active streams by folding the single-key value into shard 0
Read path sums shards; verify totals match the legacy key during overlap
Tune K per stream tier to balance write spread vs read fan-out
Remove the legacy single-key path after all active streams migrated
Rollback: collapse shards back into one key by summing then writing the single key
testing_strategy: Fixture: simulate 100k joins and 30k leaves across sharded keys for one stream. Assert output: current() equals 70k. Assert side-effect: increments distribute across all K sub-keys (no single key holds the majority), verified by reading each shard. Chaos test: kill a client mid-increment and re-run; assert no double counting beyond the single lost op tolerance and that current() stays within the accuracy bound; verify the migrate fold doesn't double-count when run twice (idempotency via del).
what_breaks_naive_approach: A single counter key forces every INCR/DECR onto one hash slot and shard, so a viral stream saturates that shard's CPU and degrades every other key colocated on it.

title: Migrate eventual-consistency dual-writes to a saga with compensations for cross-service orders
business_context: An e-commerce checkout writes to order-service, payment-service, and inventory-service via best-effort sequential HTTP calls. When inventory reservation fails after payment, the system leaves charged-but-unfulfilled orders; support handles ~300 such cases weekly. They must introduce a saga with compensating transactions for reliable cross-service consistency.
constraints: ~300 charged-but-unfulfilled orders/week from partial failures
Three services, no distributed transaction available
Compensations must be idempotent and retried until success
Checkout latency budget 800ms p95
Kafka for saga events
Rollback to old flow must be possible

before_state.description: Sequential best-effort cross-service writes with no compensation on partial failure.

issues: No compensation: a failed reserve leaves a charged order
No retry/idempotency on the cross-service steps
Manual support cleanup of ~300 cases/week

reasoning: Naive approach: wrap the three calls in a try/catch and call refund on failure inline — the inline refund itself can fail (network blip) and there's no durable retry, so charged-unfulfilled states still leak. Rejected alternative: a 2-phase-commit coordinator across the three services — rejected because the services are independently deployed and don't support XA, and 2PC blocks under coordinator failure. Chosen path: an orchestrated saga driven by Kafka events with explicit compensations (refund, release) that are idempotent and retried with a dead-letter for manual review only after exhausting retries. Cross-layer trace: checkout emits a saga-start event -> orchestrator advances order->charge->reserve, persisting each step -> on reserve failure it emits compensations (refund) which are idempotent and retried -> a saga state table tracks progress for recovery -> config: retry/backoff -> tests assert charged orders are always refunded on failure.

after_state.description: A persisted saga orchestrator advances steps and runs idempotent compensations with durable retries on failure.

files.purpose: Persists each saga's current step so the orchestrator can recover and resume
Orchestrates the forward steps and triggers compensation on reserve failure
Runs the refund idempotently so repeated retries never double-refund
Guarantees compensations eventually run, replacing manual cleanup with durable retries

migration_strategy: Make each service call idempotent (idempotency keys); add saga_state table
Route checkout through the saga orchestrator behind a flag for a canary cohort
Verify compensations refund/release correctly on injected reserve failures
Ramp saga to 100%; monitor compensation success and dead-letter rate
Decommission the old sequential flow once charged-unfulfilled rate hits zero
Rollback: flip flag to the old flow; saga_state is additive and harmless
testing_strategy: Fixture: orders where inventory reserve is forced to fail. Assert output: checkout returns failure and the order ends COMPENSATED. Assert side-effect: payment-service received exactly one charge and exactly one refund (idempotency keys prevent duplicates), net zero charge. Chaos test: make the first refund attempt fail, then succeed on retry, and assert the saga_state reaches COMPENSATED with no double refund and no manual ticket created.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Inline try/catch refunds have no durability or retry, so a transient failure in the compensating call itself leaves the order charged-but-unfulfilled, exactly the case support handles 300 times a week.

title: Online-migrate a unique constraint to a different column set after a business rule change
business_context: A SaaS originally enforced UNIQUE(email) on `users`, but a new B2B feature lets the same person belong to multiple organizations, so uniqueness must change to UNIQUE(org_id, email). 30M users exist with duplicate emails across orgs already blocked by the old constraint, requiring careful migration without dropping protection.
constraints: Must switch UNIQUE(email) to UNIQUE(org_id, email)
No window where duplicate emails within the same org slip in
30M users; 1.5k signups/sec
Existing single-org users must remain valid
Postgres 14
Rollback must restore a working uniqueness guarantee

before_state.description: Global email uniqueness blocks legitimate cross-org users.

issues: UNIQUE(email) blocks the same person joining two orgs
Business now needs per-org uniqueness
Cannot just drop the constraint without a replacement gap
Relies on the global unique constraint for protection

reasoning: Naive approach: DROP the old unique then ADD the new one — between the two statements there is a window with no uniqueness, and the new ADD scans 30M rows under a lock and could fail if any in-org duplicates exist. Rejected alternative: enforce per-org uniqueness only in app code during the swap — rejected because concurrent signups race past app checks. Chosen path: build the new UNIQUE(org_id, email) index CONCURRENTLY first (so protection overlaps), verify no in-org duplicates, then drop the old constraint. Cross-layer trace: create.js unchanged -> new partial-free unique index built CONCURRENTLY while old constraint still active -> a pre-check finds any in-org dup emails for cleanup -> drop old constraint only after the new unique index is valid -> config: none -> tests assert no uniqueness gap during the swap.

after_state.description: New UNIQUE(org_id,email) built concurrently and validated before the old global unique is dropped, so protection never lapses.

files.content:
-- find in-org duplicates that would block the new constraint
SELECT org_id, email, count(*) FROM users GROUP BY org_id, email HAVING count(*) > 1;
files.purpose: Surfaces any in-org duplicate emails to reconcile before adding the new constraint

files.content:
-- build new uniqueness CONCURRENTLY while the old one still protects
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY uq_users_org_email ON users(org_id, email);
files.purpose: Establishes the new per-org uniqueness without a protection gap
Promotes the new index to a constraint, then removes the old global one
Uses the new per-org unique constraint and idempotent insert

migration_strategy: Run the in-org duplicate pre-check and reconcile any conflicts
Build UNIQUE(org_id,email) index CONCURRENTLY while the old constraint stays active
Promote the new index to a constraint via ADD CONSTRAINT USING INDEX
Drop the old UNIQUE(email) constraint only after the new one is in force
Update create() to ON CONFLICT(org_id,email); allow cross-org duplicate emails
Rollback: keep the old constraint until the new index is valid; drop the new index to revert
testing_strategy: Fixture: 1M users; include one person's email present in two different orgs (previously blocked). Assert output: create() now allows the same email in a second org but rejects a duplicate within the same org. Assert side-effect: at every step there is at least one active uniqueness guarantee (verify by attempting an in-org duplicate during the swap and confirming it's rejected). Chaos test: fire concurrent signups with the same (org_id,email) during the index build and assert exactly one wins.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Dropping the old unique before the new one is in force opens a window where concurrent signups insert in-org duplicate emails, and a single-statement ADD of the new constraint can fail mid-scan on existing duplicates.

title: Migrate to logical replication to upgrade Postgres major version with near-zero downtime
business_context: A fintech runs Postgres 12 and must upgrade to 16 for performance and security, but a pg_upgrade in place needs a maintenance window their 24/7 trading platform can't take. The 1.2TB database serves 18k queries/sec. They must use logical replication to a new PG16 instance and cut over with seconds of downtime.
constraints: 1.2TB database; near-zero downtime cutover
18k queries/sec, 4k writes/sec
Sequences must be advanced correctly on cutover
All tables must have a replica identity for logical replication
PG12 -> PG16
Rollback to PG12 must be possible immediately after cutover

before_state.description: PG12 primary; in-place pg_upgrade requires an unacceptable maintenance window.

files.content:
pg_upgrade -b /pg12/bin -B /pg16/bin -d /pg12/data -D /pg16/data --link
# requires stopping the primary for the duration
issues: pg_upgrade stops the primary; no acceptable window
Large catalog and 1.2TB make the link step risky
No incremental cutover
Tables without PK lack a replica identity for logical replication

reasoning: Naive approach: pg_upgrade --link during a short window — even --link needs the primary stopped and a risky catalog conversion on a 1.2TB DB; the platform can't stop. Rejected alternative: streaming (physical) replication to a PG16 standby — rejected because physical replication can't span major versions. Chosen path: logical replication from PG12 to a fresh PG16, ensure every table has a replica identity, initial-copy + stream changes, then a brief cutover that stops writes, drains lag, advances sequences, and repoints the app. Cross-layer trace: app keeps hitting PG12 -> a PUBLICATION streams all tables to PG16 -> tables lacking PK get REPLICA IDENTITY FULL -> at cutover writes pause, replication lag drains to zero, sequences are set on PG16, app DSN flips -> config: connection string -> tests assert row parity and sequence correctness.

after_state.description: Logical replication copies and streams PG12 to PG16; cutover pauses writes briefly, drains lag, advances sequences, and repoints the app.

files.content:
-- tables without PK need an identity for logical replication
ALTER TABLE legacy_audit REPLICA IDENTITY FULL;
files.purpose: Ensures every table can be replicated logically, including those without a primary key
Performs the initial copy and then streams ongoing changes to PG16

files.content:
set -e
# 1. stop writes (set app to read-only / pause writers)
# 2. wait until pg_stat_subscription lag = 0 on PG16
# 3. advance sequences
psql pg16 -f advance_sequences.sql
# 4. flip app DSN to PG16, resume writes
kubectl set env deploy/api DATABASE_URL=$PG16_URL
files.purpose: Executes a seconds-long cutover after draining replication lag

files.content:
-- logical replication does NOT copy sequence positions; set them from max(id)
SELECT setval('trades_id_seq', (SELECT max(id) FROM trades));
files.purpose: Advances sequences on PG16 so post-cutover inserts don't collide with replicated rows

migration_strategy: Provision PG16; set REPLICA IDENTITY FULL on PK-less tables
Create publication on PG12 and subscription on PG16 (initial copy + stream)
Monitor pg_stat_subscription until lag is steadily near zero
Cutover: pause writes, drain to zero lag, advance all sequences, flip app DSN
Resume writes on PG16; keep PG12 streaming-capable as immediate fallback
Rollback: re-point app to PG12 (still has all data) if PG16 misbehaves post-cutover
testing_strategy: Fixture: dataset with PK and PK-less tables and active sequences. Assert output: row counts and checksums match between PG12 and PG16 after sync. Assert side-effect: after advance_sequences, the next insert on PG16 gets an id greater than max(id) (no collision with replicated rows). Chaos test: insert during the drain and assert the change replicates before cutover completes; force a PK-less table change and assert REPLICA IDENTITY FULL lets it replicate, unlike the default which would error.
what_breaks_naive_approach: pg_upgrade requires stopping the primary which a 24/7 platform can't allow, and physical replication can't bridge a major-version gap; logical replication also silently skips sequence positions, so forgetting to advance them causes id collisions after cutover.

title: Migrate a denormalized address blob to a normalized addresses table with backfill validation
business_context: A logistics platform stored shipping addresses as a free-text `address` string on `shipments`. Geocoding and route optimization now need structured fields (street, city, postal, country), but 250M historical shipments have only the blob. They must normalize into an `addresses` table, parse legacy blobs, and flag unparseable ones for manual review without blocking 5k shipment creates/sec.
constraints: 250M legacy shipments with free-text address only
5k creates/sec must not be blocked
Unparseable addresses must be flagged, not dropped
Parsing must be idempotent and resumable
Postgres 14
Rollback must keep the original blob intact

before_state.description: Free-text address blob can't be queried by structured fields.

issues: Address is a single free-text field
No structured city/postal/country for geocoding
No way to index or validate components
Stores only the raw blob; downstream geocoding must re-parse each time

reasoning: Naive approach: parse the blob in a single big UPDATE adding columns — a 250M-row UPDATE with a parser locks and bloats, and unparseable rows would either fail or get garbage. Rejected alternative: parse lazily at read time only — rejected because route optimization needs to query/index by postal and country, which lazy parsing can't index. Chosen path: add a normalized addresses table, parse new shipments at write time into structured fields, backfill legacy blobs in idempotent batches, and route unparseable rows to a review queue. Cross-layer trace: create.js parses and writes structured fields + keeps the blob -> addresses table holds components with an FK -> backfill worker parses legacy blobs in batches keyed by id, marking parsed=true -> failures go to address_review -> config: parser confidence threshold -> tests assert parse correctness and review routing.

after_state.description: Normalized addresses table populated at write time and via idempotent backfill; unparseable legacy blobs flagged for review; original blob retained.

files.purpose: Stores structured, indexable address components linked to the shipment
Parses new addresses at write time, keeps the blob, and queues unparseable ones
Backfills legacy blobs idempotently in keyset batches, flagging failures for review
Holds unparseable addresses for manual correction instead of silently dropping them

migration_strategy: Create addresses and address_review tables
Deploy write-time parsing that keeps the blob and queues failures
Backfill legacy shipments in keyset batches with ON CONFLICT idempotency
Build geocoding indexes on (country, postal) once backfill is mostly done
Hand the review queue to ops; resolved rows update the addresses table
Rollback: addresses is additive; original address blob is untouched
testing_strategy: Fixture: 100k shipments including well-formed and malformed address blobs. Assert output: parseable addresses populate structured fields correctly; malformed ones land in address_review. Assert side-effect: every shipment has exactly one addresses row (parsed true/false) and unparseable ones appear once in review (ON CONFLICT prevents dupes). Chaos test: run the backfill twice and kill it mid-batch; assert no duplicate addresses or review rows and that the original blob is never modified.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A single 250M-row parsing UPDATE locks and bloats the table and has no graceful path for unparseable blobs, which would either abort the migration or store garbage in the structured columns.

title: Migrate a polymorphic association to explicit FK tables for referential integrity
business_context: A devtools app uses a polymorphic `comments(commentable_type, commentable_id)` pattern referencing issues, PRs, or commits. There are no FKs so deleting a parent orphans comments; 14k orphaned comments accumulated and a report crashed dereferencing them. They must move to explicit per-type FK columns without breaking the 9 services reading comments.
constraints: No FK possible on polymorphic columns -> 14k orphans
9 services read comments by (type,id)
60M comments, 2k writes/sec
Deleting a parent must cascade or restrict, not orphan
Postgres 14
Rollback must keep polymorphic reads working

before_state.description: Polymorphic association prevents FKs, allowing orphaned comments.

issues: No FK can reference a polymorphic (type,id) pair
Deleting an issue orphans its comments
14k orphans crash a dereferencing report
Reads by (type,id) with no integrity guarantee

reasoning: Naive approach: add a CHECK and triggers to validate the polymorphic reference — triggers can't be enforced atomically against concurrent parent deletes and add per-write overhead. Rejected alternative: separate comment tables per parent type (issue_comments, pr_comments) — rejected because it shatters the 9 services' single-table reads and cross-type listing. Chosen path: keep one comments table but add nullable typed FK columns (issue_id, pr_id, commit_id), each with a real FK and a CHECK that exactly one is set, backfill from the polymorphic pair, and keep a compatibility view exposing (type,id). Cross-layer trace: read.js can keep using (type,id) via a generated view -> writes set the correct typed FK column -> backfill maps polymorphic rows to typed columns -> FKs enforce integrity and cascade -> orphans quarantined -> tests assert deleting a parent no longer orphans.

after_state.description: Typed nullable FK columns with a one-of CHECK enforce integrity; a view preserves the polymorphic read shape.

files.content:
ALTER TABLE comments
  ADD COLUMN issue_id BIGINT REFERENCES issues(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  ADD COLUMN pr_id BIGINT REFERENCES prs(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  ADD COLUMN commit_id BIGINT REFERENCES commits(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  ADD CONSTRAINT one_parent CHECK (
    (issue_id IS NOT NULL)::int + (pr_id IS NOT NULL)::int + (commit_id IS NOT NULL)::int = 1);
files.purpose: Adds real FKs per parent type with a CHECK ensuring exactly one parent, enabling cascade
Maps polymorphic references into the typed FK columns in batches

files.content:
CREATE VIEW comments_poly AS
SELECT id, body,
  CASE WHEN issue_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'issue' WHEN pr_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'pr' ELSE 'commit' END AS commentable_type,
  COALESCE(issue_id, pr_id, commit_id) AS commentable_id
FROM comments;
files.purpose: Preserves the (type,id) read shape so the 9 services migrate gradually
Writes the correct typed FK column so new comments are integrity-protected

migration_strategy: Quarantine the 14k orphans, then add typed FK columns and the one-of CHECK
Backfill typed columns from the polymorphic pair in id batches
Create comments_poly view; migrate the 9 services to it or to typed columns
Switch writes to set typed FK columns; FKs now cascade on parent delete
Drop commentable_type/commentable_id after all readers move off them
Rollback: keep polymorphic columns until cutover; FK columns are additive
testing_strategy: Fixture: comments across issues/PRs/commits plus orphans. Assert output: comments_poly returns the same (type,id) shape as the old table. Assert side-effect: deleting an issue cascades and removes its comments (no orphans), verified by count before/after. Negative test: attempt to insert a comment with two typed FK columns set and assert the one_parent CHECK rejects it; attempt a comment referencing a nonexistent issue and assert the FK rejects it.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Polymorphic (type,id) columns can't carry a foreign key, so a parent delete silently orphans comments; validation triggers can't atomically prevent this under concurrent deletes.

title: Migrate a hot append table to BRIN indexing to reclaim space from oversized B-trees
business_context: An observability platform stores 6B log lines in a time-ordered `logs` table. A B-tree on `ts` is 180GB and dominates disk and write cost, yet queries always scan time ranges where data is naturally clustered. They must replace the B-tree with a BRIN index to cut index size ~99% without slowing range queries or blocking 30k inserts/sec.
constraints: B-tree on ts is 180GB; disk pressure
30k log inserts/sec, append-only, naturally time-ordered
Range queries by ts must stay fast
BRIN correctness depends on physical clustering
Postgres 14
Rollback must restore a usable ts index

before_state.description: A huge B-tree on a naturally clustered timestamp wastes space and write effort.

files.content:
CREATE TABLE logs (
  id BIGINT,
  ts TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
  level TEXT,
  msg TEXT
);
CREATE INDEX idx_logs_ts ON logs(ts); -- 180GB B-tree
issues: B-tree on ts is 180GB on a 6B-row append table
Every insert updates the large B-tree
Data is already physically time-ordered, so B-tree precision is wasted
Range scan uses an oversized B-tree

reasoning: Naive approach: just drop the B-tree to save space — range queries then seq-scan 6B rows. Rejected alternative: keep the B-tree but reduce fillfactor — rejected because it doesn't shrink the 180GB and still pays full per-insert maintenance. Chosen path: build a BRIN index on ts (tiny because it stores per-block-range min/max), which works precisely because the append-only table is physically clustered by time, then drop the B-tree. Cross-layer trace: query.js unchanged -> BRIN gives block-range pruning for time queries at ~1/1000th the size -> insert cost drops since BRIN maintenance is cheap -> verify clustering correlation is near 1 (required for BRIN to help) -> build BRIN, validate plans, drop B-tree -> config: pages_per_range tuned -> tests assert range queries use BRIN and stay fast.

after_state.description: A tiny BRIN index replaces the 180GB B-tree, leveraging the table's physical time clustering for range pruning.

files.purpose: Builds a compact BRIN index that prunes block ranges for time queries

files.content:
-- confirm ts is highly correlated with physical order (required for BRIN)
SELECT correlation FROM pg_stats WHERE tablename='logs' AND attname='ts';
files.purpose: Verifies the physical clustering BRIN depends on before dropping the B-tree

files.content:
-- only after BRIN proven on canary range queries
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_logs_ts;
files.purpose: Reclaims 180GB and removes per-insert B-tree maintenance
Same query, now served by BRIN block-range pruning

migration_strategy: Confirm pg_stats correlation on ts is near 1 (BRIN viability)
Build the BRIN index CONCURRENTLY with tuned pages_per_range
Validate range-query plans use the BRIN and latency is acceptable on a canary
Drop the 180GB B-tree CONCURRENTLY to reclaim disk
Monitor query latency and insert throughput over 48h
Rollback: rebuild the B-tree CONCURRENTLY if BRIN underperforms
testing_strategy: Fixture: 20M time-ordered log rows. Assert output: range() returns the correct rows for a time window. Assert side-effect: idx_logs_ts_brin size is orders of magnitude smaller than the B-tree (pg_relation_size) and EXPLAIN shows a BRIN Bitmap Index Scan with effective range pruning. Negative test: insert deliberately out-of-order timestamps to degrade correlation and assert the BRIN recheck still returns correct results (correctness preserved) while documenting the latency cost of poor clustering.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Dropping the B-tree without a replacement forces seq scans over 6B rows for every time range, and keeping the B-tree leaves 180GB and full per-insert maintenance that BRIN avoids.

title: Migrate cross-shard aggregate reads to a denormalized read model via CQRS
business_context: A logistics dashboard needs fleet-wide aggregates (total parcels in transit per region) but data is sharded by carrier across 12 Postgres instances. Scatter-gather queries across shards take 7s and time out during peaks. They must build a denormalized read model fed by shard change events so the dashboard reads one place.
constraints: Aggregates span 12 carrier shards; scatter-gather takes 7s
Dashboard must read aggregates in <300ms
Read model must be eventually consistent within 5s
Writes stay on the shards (source of truth)
Kafka events per shard
Rollback to scatter-gather must be possible

before_state.description: Dashboard scatter-gathers aggregates across 12 shards, timing out.

issues: Scatter-gather across 12 shards; slowest shard dominates (7s)
Times out during peaks
App-side merge is fragile

reasoning: Naive approach: cache the scatter-gather result for 5s — still pays the 7s cost on cache miss and a slow shard can stall the whole dashboard. Rejected alternative: a nightly batch into a warehouse — rejected because the dashboard needs near-real-time (5s) aggregates, not day-old. Chosen path: CQRS read model in a separate aggregate store updated incrementally from per-shard parcel status-change events, so the dashboard reads pre-aggregated counts. Cross-layer trace: each shard emits parcel status-change events -> a projector consumes them and maintains region/status counters in a read-model table -> dashboard reads the read model in one query -> a periodic reconciliation recomputes from shards to correct drift -> config: read-model store -> tests assert read model equals scatter-gather totals.

after_state.description: A CQRS read model maintains region/status counters from shard events; the dashboard reads one pre-aggregated table.

files.purpose: Stores pre-aggregated counts the dashboard reads in a single fast query
Maintains counters incrementally and idempotently from shard status-change events
Reads pre-aggregated counts in one query under the latency budget
Periodically recomputes truth from shards to correct any projection drift

migration_strategy: Stand up the read-model table and projector consuming shard status events
Seed the read model via a full reconcile from all shards
Run dashboard reads against the read model behind a flag; compare to scatter-gather
Cut the dashboard to the read model; keep scatter-gather as fallback
Schedule periodic reconcile to bound drift
Rollback: flip the dashboard back to scatter-gather (shards are source of truth)
testing_strategy: Fixture: parcels across 12 simulated shards with known region/status distribution. Assert output: inTransitByRegion from the read model equals the scatter-gather totals. Assert side-effect: replaying the same event batch twice leaves counters unchanged (projected_events dedupe). Chaos test: drop a batch of events to induce drift, run reconcile, and assert the read model converges back to shard truth; verify a slow shard no longer affects dashboard latency.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Caching the scatter-gather still pays the 7s cost on every cache miss and remains hostage to the slowest of 12 shards, so peak-time timeouts persist.

title: Migrate a non-deterministic ORDER BY to a stable sort to fix flaky pagination and exports
business_context: A fintech reporting export paginates transactions ORDER BY amount, but many rows share the same amount, so Postgres returns ties in arbitrary order that changes between runs. Exports occasionally duplicate or skip rows across pages, and an auditor flagged inconsistent reports. They must make ordering deterministic without breaking the 90M-row export performance.
constraints: Ties on amount cause arbitrary, run-varying order
Paginated exports must be stable across pages and runs
90M transactions; export must stay performant
Existing API contract sorts by amount
Postgres 14
Rollback to prior ordering must be safe

before_state.description: ORDER BY a non-unique column yields arbitrary tie ordering, breaking pagination.

issues: ORDER BY amount only; ties ordered arbitrarily
Cursor on amount can't disambiguate equal amounts
Rows duplicated/skipped across pages between runs

reasoning: Naive approach: add ORDER BY amount, random() to shuffle ties consistently — random() is non-deterministic per execution, making it worse.
