Rejected alternative: sort in the application after fetching all rows — rejected because 90M rows can't be materialized in app memory. Chosen path: make the sort total by appending the unique id as a tiebreaker and switch pagination to a composite keyset cursor on (amount, id), backed by a matching index. Cross-layer trace: page.js orders by (amount, id) so ties are deterministic -> the cursor is the composite (amount, id) pair -> a (amount, id) index makes it an index range scan -> exports become stable across pages and runs -> config: none -> tests assert no duplicate/skipped rows across runs.

after_state.description: Total ordering via (amount, id) with a composite keyset cursor and supporting index yields stable, performant pagination.

files.content: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_tx_amount_id ON transactions(amount, id);
files.purpose: Supports the deterministic (amount,id) ordering as an index range scan
Uses a total (amount,id) order and composite keyset so pages never overlap or skip

files.content: function nextCursor(rows){const last=rows[rows.length-1];return last?{amount:last.amount,id:last.id}:null;}
files.purpose: Builds the composite cursor from the last row's (amount,id)

migration_strategy: Build the (amount,id) composite index CONCURRENTLY
Switch the export to ORDER BY amount, id with the composite keyset cursor
Run a full export twice and diff to confirm identical, stable output
Roll out to all export consumers; deprecate amount-only cursors
Add a regression test asserting deterministic ordering
Rollback: revert to amount-only ordering (index is harmless to keep)
testing_strategy: Fixture: transactions with many ties on amount. Assert output: paging through the full set yields every row exactly once with no duplicates or skips. Assert side-effect: EXPLAIN shows an Index Scan on idx_tx_amount_id (no separate Sort node). Chaos test: run the full paginated export twice concurrently and assert byte-identical ordered output, proving determinism despite ties, unlike the amount-only version which diverges.
what_breaks_naive_approach: ORDER BY a non-unique column leaves tie ordering to the executor, so equal-amount rows shuffle between runs and a cursor on amount alone can't tell them apart, duplicating or skipping rows across pages.

title: Migrate a synchronous fan-out write to an outbox-driven async projection for write latency
business_context: A social app's post-create path synchronously writes the post, updates each follower's feed table (fan-out on write), and bumps counters in one request. Users with 200k followers see 9s create latency and timeouts. They must move fan-out to an async outbox-driven projection so create returns fast while feeds populate reliably.
constraints: Celebrity posts fan out to 200k+ followers; 9s create latency
Create must return in <200ms
No follower's feed may miss a post (at-least-once delivery)
Idempotent feed inserts (no duplicate feed entries)
Postgres + Kafka outbox
Rollback to synchronous fan-out must be possible

before_state.description: Synchronous fan-out in the create request causes multi-second latency for high-follower users.

issues: Fan-out to 200k followers inline -> 9s latency, timeouts
One slow insert stalls the whole request
No retry if the request dies mid-fan-out

reasoning: Naive approach: spawn an async background promise for the fan-out and return early — if the process crashes the in-memory fan-out is lost and those followers miss the post. Rejected alternative: a pure fan-out-on-read model — rejected as a larger redesign than this milestone allows and changes feed query patterns the app depends on. Chosen path: write the post and a fan-out outbox event in one transaction, return immediately, and let an outbox consumer perform idempotent batched feed inserts with retries. Cross-layer trace: create.js writes post + outbox row atomically and returns -> consumer reads the event and inserts feed rows in batches -> feed inserts are idempotent (unique on user_id,post_id) -> retries on failure ensure at-least-once -> config: batch size -> tests assert all followers eventually get the post exactly once.

after_state.description: Create writes post + outbox event transactionally and returns fast; an async consumer fans out idempotently with retries.

files.purpose: Persists the post and a fan-out event atomically and returns in well under 200ms
Unique (user_id,post_id) makes feed inserts idempotent against retries
Fans out in idempotent batches so retries never duplicate feed entries
Retries fan-out until success so no follower permanently misses the post

migration_strategy: Add unique (user_id,post_id) on feeds for idempotency
Deploy transactional post+outbox write and the fan-out consumer behind a flag
Canary celebrity accounts; verify create latency drops and feeds fully populate
Ramp to all posts; remove the synchronous fan-out path
Monitor consumer lag and dead-letter rate; alert on backlog
Rollback: re-enable synchronous fan-out; outbox events are additive
testing_strategy: Fixture: an author with 200k followers. Assert output: create() returns under the latency budget. Assert side-effect: after the consumer drains, all 200k followers have exactly one feed row for the post (idempotent). Chaos test: kill the consumer mid-fan-out and restart; assert ON CONFLICT prevents duplicate feed rows and retries complete the remaining followers, so none miss the post.
what_breaks_naive_approach: Firing the fan-out as an in-memory background promise loses the remaining inserts if the process crashes, so a large fraction of followers silently never receive the post.
